task_prompt:
A streaming platform is reviewing a new offline recommendation stack for homepage videos. The legacy system uses a matrix-factorization candidate generator followed by a gradient boosted reranker. The proposed replacement introduces a two-tower retrieval model and a listwise transformer reranker. In the experiment report, the team compares the legacy stack's NDCG@20 against the new stack's NDCG@20 on a replay dataset built from impression logs. The new stack wins by about 4.5% relative, and the summary slide presents this as a model improvement.

The details section explains that the two-tower retriever returns 500 candidates per user, whereas the legacy retriever returned 200, because the new infrastructure can score more items. The replay evaluation then scores the reranker only on the candidates returned by each system's own retriever. As a result, the transformer reranker is evaluated on the newer 500-item candidate pool and the baseline reranker is evaluated on the legacy 200-item candidate pool. The team did not include a factorial ablation because the retrieval and ranking changes were launched together in code review. They also mention that the evaluation uses a leave-last-week-out split and only 5% of eligible users because older impression logs expire after 30 days, but the sampled users were drawn uniformly from all traffic segments.

Product leadership wants to use the offline gain as evidence that the new ranking model is stronger. Assess the benchmark design and what additional analysis would be needed before interpreting the reported lift.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The offline comparison is confounded because the candidate generator and reranker changed together and each reranker was scored on a different candidate pool.

planted_issues.description: Each reranker is evaluated on the items retrieved by its own generator, so the reported NDCG mixes retrieval quality with ranking quality.
The challenger receives 500 retrieved candidates while the baseline receives 200, which changes the evaluation opportunity set.
Because retrieval and reranking changed simultaneously without a common-candidate ablation, the benchmark cannot attribute the gain to the ranking model.

ideal_critique: Notice that the metric is not isolating the reranker because the opportunity set differs by system.
Call out the increased candidate depth as part of the protocol change, not merely an engineering detail.
Recommend evaluating both rerankers on a frozen shared candidate pool and separately measuring candidate-generator recall.
ideal_defense: Concede that leave-last-week-out and using a representative 5% user sample are not the key flaws by themselves.
Argue only that the full stack may still be better operationally, but the current benchmark does not prove the reranker alone is better.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: On a frozen common 500-item candidate pool, the transformer reranker loses the reported 4.5% relative NDCG gain or the gain is fully explained by retrieval recall differences.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: With both rerankers evaluated on the same frozen candidate pool and both generators compared at the same depth, the transformer still improves NDCG@20 by at least 2% relative and the new retriever also improves recall separately.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The common-pool reranker gain is positive but under 2% relative while the new retriever contributes most of the stack-level improvement.

must_not_claim: Using leave-last-week-out in recommendation is automatically leakage.
Evaluating on only 5% of users is invalid even if the sample is representative and large.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a broken-baseline case about confounding model quality with a changed retrieval pipeline and candidate depth. The time-based split and sampled-user evaluation are intentionally suspicious-looking but can be perfectly valid if the sample is representative and the split respects chronology.
notes: Medium case with red herrings: leave-last-week-out and a representative 5% user sample are valid. Core flaw is that the new reranker is not benchmarked on the same candidate set as the baseline. Added empirical_test_agreed as secondary resolution: the baseline flaw could be confirmed by re-running with corrected evaluation. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt:
A pharmacy chain is evaluating a new demand-forecasting system for store-item replenishment. The incumbent baseline is an ARIMA-based model maintained by the supply-planning team, and the proposed replacement is a gradient boosting forecast that uses lagged demand, holiday calendars, and promotion plans. The project summary reports that the new model improves weighted absolute percentage error over the baseline by roughly 7% and recommends a phased rollout to all stores.

In the methodology appendix, the team notes that the data engineering group corrected several historical unit-conversion problems after discovering that some pack sizes were recorded inconsistently across vendors. They also backfilled known stockout days using neighboring-store patterns so the learning target reflects latent demand rather than observed zero sales during outages. Those corrections were applied in the new pipeline before training and evaluation. The baseline number, however, is copied from the prior replenishment dashboard, which was computed on the older raw sales series before unit normalization and stockout backfilling. In addition, the dashboard baseline was tracked for one-day-ahead forecasts, whereas the new project reports seven-day-ahead aggregate WAPE because planners place weekly orders. The report also mentions that only 18 months of history were available for some recently acquired stores and that evaluation used rolling-origin backtesting rather than a random split.

Operations leaders want to use the reported WAPE improvement to justify replacing the incumbent forecast. Review whether the benchmark comparison is valid and what a fair comparison would require.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The benchmark is broken because the challenger and baseline were evaluated on different target preprocessing and different forecast horizons.

planted_issues.description: The challenger is evaluated on a corrected latent-demand target while the baseline number comes from the older raw sales series, so the target itself differs across systems.
Applying stockout backfilling and unit normalization only in the challenger pipeline confounds the reported gain with preprocessing improvements.
The baseline was tracked one day ahead while the challenger is reported on seven-day-ahead weekly ordering targets, so the error numbers are not directly comparable.

ideal_critique: Identify that the benchmark changes both the target series and the forecast horizon, so the comparison is not apples to apples.
Recognize that stockout correction can be appropriate operationally but must be applied consistently to both systems when benchmarking.
Recommend rerunning the incumbent on the corrected series and matching horizon and metric definitions.
ideal_defense: Concede that rolling-origin backtesting and limited history for newly acquired stores are not the main problems here.
Argue only that the new pipeline may be better for the actual planning task, but the current benchmark does not isolate model value from target and horizon changes.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After both systems are rerun on the same corrected latent-demand series at the same seven-day horizon, the reported 7% WAPE improvement falls below 2% or disappears.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Using the same corrected series and the same seven-day horizon for both systems still leaves the gradient boosting model ahead by at least 5% relative WAPE across stores.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A fair rerun leaves a 2% to 5% relative WAPE gain overall but reversals in key store segments or product classes.

must_not_claim: Rolling-origin backtesting is invalid because the split is not random.
Using planned promotions as features is necessarily future leakage even when promotions are known before orders are placed.

ground_truth_rationale: This case is not about whether latent-demand correction is conceptually good or bad; it may be operationally sensible. The benchmark fails because those corrections and the forecast horizon were changed only for the challenger, so the score gap cannot be attributed to the model alone.
notes: Medium case with red herrings: rolling-origin backtesting and limited history for some stores are valid choices. The real flaw is preprocessing and horizon mismatch between baseline and challenger. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt:
A payments company is reviewing a fraud model before rollout to card-not-present traffic. The modeling team reports that the new system improves offline ROC-AUC from 0.948 to 0.967 on the last six months of labeled transactions and says in the rollout proposal that it should "cut chargebacks by 25% without materially increasing customer friction." Fraud labels arrive only after disputes and investigations, often six to eight weeks after the original transaction. The live system uses a score threshold plus a manual-review queue, and the business has little tolerance for false declines on repeat customers.

The evaluation packet includes ROC and precision-recall curves for the full validation set and shows that the new model is better almost everywhere on those curves. It does not include a threshold-specific simulation of approvals, manual-review load, merchant mix, or customer segment effects under the proposed operating policy. It also does not test how the model performs after intervention, when fraudsters adapt and when some suspicious transactions are blocked before labels can mature. The team argues that because AUC is substantially higher and the gain appears in multiple monthly backtests, the online result should follow. No live shadow test or randomized traffic split has been run yet.

Your task is to evaluate whether the reported evidence supports the proposal's business claim. Focus on whether the metric emphasized in the packet is the right one for the specific promised outcomes, and whether any defensible partial argument exists in favor of the rollout. You may also discuss what additional testing would resolve the disagreement.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Higher offline ROC-AUC alone does not justify a claim about chargeback reduction with minimal customer-friction impact in a live fraud system.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run threshold-specific policy simulation and then a shadow or randomized deployment measuring chargebacks, false declines, and manual-review load under the actual operating policy.

planted_issues.description: ROC-AUC is threshold-free and does not directly measure the business tradeoff between chargebacks, false declines, and review load.
Offline performance on matured historical labels may not carry over online because labels are delayed and fraud patterns change after intervention.
The proposal lacks threshold-specific analysis of the actual decision policy, including review capacity and customer-friction constraints.

ideal_critique: Distinguish a ranking improvement from the stronger promise about business outcomes under a thresholded, capacity-limited decision system.
Explain why label delay and fraud adaptation weaken the offline-to-online link.
Ask for thresholded simulations and live evaluation of chargebacks, false declines, and review burden.
ideal_defense: Concede that higher AUC is encouraging but not sufficient for the proposal's business claim.
Argue that repeated monthly backtests make the model worth a carefully instrumented shadow or limited rollout.
Avoid presenting offline AUC as if it directly translates into the promised online tradeoff.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: At the proposed threshold, live or shadow evaluation fails to reduce chargebacks by the claimed amount or does so only by causing unacceptable false declines or review overload.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Under the actual operating policy, randomized or shadow deployment shows the claimed chargeback reduction with false declines and review load staying within predefined limits.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves chargebacks, but only with a customer-friction or queue-cost tradeoff that the proposal did not specify.

must_not_claim: roc_auc_is_never_useful
multiple_monthly_backtests_are_meaningless

ground_truth_rationale: The proposed business claim is about live policy outcomes, but ROC-AUC is a threshold-free offline ranking metric. In fraud settings, delayed labels, adaptive adversaries, and review-capacity constraints make the offline-to-online gap especially important.
notes: Medium offline-to-online fraud case. Strong backtests are intentionally included as useful but insufficient evidence. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt:
A retail analytics vendor is summarizing a computer vision pilot for shelf monitoring. The system uses overhead store cameras to flag likely out-of-stock facings for packaged snacks and beverages. In the report, the vendor compares manual audit discrepancy rates from August and September, when the model was active in 30 stores near large universities, with rates from May and June in those same stores before the pilot. They conclude that the model reduced missed stockouts by 22% and recommend chain-wide rollout.

The report explains that the pilot stores were chosen because they already had camera coverage and store managers who would cooperate with manual audits. It also notes that the chain refreshed planograms and end-cap layouts in late July ahead of the back-to-school season. Traffic in these stores typically rises when students return, especially for snacks and cold drinks, but the report does not include stores outside the pilot group or a prior-year comparison for the same months. All pilot stores used the same camera mounting standard, and the model backbone had been pretrained on a large public retail-image dataset before fine-tuning on store-specific images.

The vendor emphasizes that audit procedures were consistent across periods and that the observed improvement was large enough to matter operationally.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The before-versus-after result is confounded by seasonal demand changes and a simultaneous store-layout refresh, so the claimed model effect is not established.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compare pilot and non-pilot stores over the same months, or use a prior-year same-period baseline with layout changes held constant.

planted_issues.description: August and September demand patterns in college-area stores differ sharply from May and June, so stockout behavior can change even without the model.
The chain changed layouts just before the pilot period, which can alter both true stockouts and how easily cameras detect them.
Without non-pilot stores or a same-period comparison, the analysis cannot separate the model from broader seasonal and operational changes.

ideal_critique: Connect the timing of the pilot to the back-to-school demand shift in exactly the store type chosen for the pilot.
Notice that the layout refresh is another concurrent operational change that affects the outcome and the vision input.
Ask for same-period controls or prior-year same-month comparisons rather than accepting a May-June baseline.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current causal claim is too strong.
Argue that the pilot may still indicate operational promise if a matched store comparison confirms the effect.
Do not attack pretraining or standardized camera mounting, because those are not the key flaws here.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Comparable non-pilot stores or prior-year same-month comparisons show similar audit changes without the model, or the lift vanishes after controlling for layout refresh timing.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The pilot stores still outperform matched control stores during the same August-September period after holding layout changes constant.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some benefit remains, but a substantial part of the before-after improvement is explained by season and layout changes.

must_not_claim: pretraining on public retail images automatically creates leakage
standardized camera mounting is itself a flaw

ground_truth_rationale: The pilot compares non-equivalent time windows in a store segment with strong seasonal swings. Because layout changes happened at almost the same time, the observed improvement cannot be confidently attributed to the vision model.
notes: Temporal confound in computer vision. Includes valid-looking suspicious features: pretraining and standardized camera setup. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt:
A telecom retention team is assessing a new churn model built with an AutoML platform. Each week, the model ranks postpaid subscribers by predicted cancellation risk. Agents then call the highest-risk customers and can offer discounts or plan changes. After two months, the team reports that contacted customers chosen by the model churned less often over the next 60 days than customers who were not contacted, and it recommends making the workflow permanent.

The operational memo says only customers eligible for retention offers were placed in the calling pool. Prepaid users, accounts already delinquent, and some enterprise lines were excluded because agents could not offer the same save actions to them. Supervisors also allowed agents to override the ranked list for VIP accounts or customers with recent service complaints. The evaluation compared churn among contacted customers versus non-contacted customers during the same period, but did not mention a randomized holdout among eligible accounts. The model used routine account, billing, and service-history features, and the team attached SHAP-style feature summaries to show which variables mattered most.

Leadership likes that the workflow seems practical and interpretable. The question is whether the observed lower churn among contacted users demonstrates that the model and intervention genuinely reduced churn.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported churn reduction is not a clean model effect because treatment and comparison groups were formed through eligibility filters and discretionary outreach rather than random assignment.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Randomize outreach or hold out a subset of eligible high-risk customers, while keeping eligibility rules and agent overrides fixed or measured.

planted_issues.description: Only accounts eligible for retention offers entered the calling pool, so contacted and non-contacted customers need not come from the same population.
Agents could override the model ranking for VIPs and recent complainants, creating manual treatment assignment correlated with churn risk.
Without a randomized holdout among comparable eligible customers, lower churn among contacted users could reflect selection rather than intervention success.

ideal_critique: Observe that contacted and non-contacted users were not generated by a clean experimental design.
Explain how eligibility rules and manual overrides can distort who enters each group.
Ask for a randomized holdout or another design that compares like with like.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current evidence is suggestive, not causal.
Argue that operational feasibility and interpretable outputs may justify a more rigorous trial.
Do not claim that AutoML or SHAP outputs answer the causal question.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A randomized holdout among eligible high-risk accounts shows much smaller or no churn reduction relative to the observational comparison.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A randomized outreach test on the ranked population produces a meaningful churn reduction under the same eligibility rules.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Some benefit appears in a cleaner test, but much of the original observational gap was due to eligibility and override patterns.

must_not_claim: using AutoML is itself evidence the result is invalid
SHAP summaries are the core methodological problem

ground_truth_rationale: The observed gap mixes model targeting, offer eligibility, and human override decisions. Because outreach was not randomized among comparable customers, the lower churn rate cannot be confidently attributed to the model-driven intervention.
notes: Medium churn/AutoML case. Includes suspicious-but-valid features in the prompt: AutoML and SHAP explanations. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt:
A regional hospital system is reviewing a machine learning alert built to estimate which admitted adult inpatients are likely to meet sepsis criteria within the next 12 hours. The model was trained on electronic health record data from three hospitals in the system and reached an AUROC of 0.87 on a held-out retrospective test set. In a later silent prospective evaluation at one hospital, where scores were generated but not shown to clinicians, AUROC was 0.84 and the median lead time before documented sepsis recognition was about five hours. The product team now wants a press release and internal funding memo that describe the system as a tool that prevents sepsis deterioration by enabling earlier treatment, and the memo cites potential reductions in ICU transfers and mortality.

During a small operational pilot, clinicians did receive alerts, but there was no mandated treatment protocol, no randomization, and no comparison unit; clinicians could act on the alert, ignore it, or already have been evaluating the patient for sepsis. The pilot dashboard mainly reported alert precision, lead time, and how often clinicians opened the sepsis order set after seeing an alert. Pediatric patients, emergency department encounters, and hospitals outside the system were excluded. The training process oversampled septic cases to address class imbalance, but evaluation used the original event rate. Some leaders argue that if the model reliably flags patients earlier than charted recognition, it is reasonable to describe it as reducing harm. Others argue the current evidence supports prediction in similar inpatient adult settings, but not intervention effects. Assess whether the proposed claim is properly scoped to the evidence described.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claim overreaches because the evidence shows earlier risk prediction in similar adult inpatient settings, not demonstrated reductions in sepsis deterioration or other clinical outcomes.

planted_issues.description: The study establishes that the model predicts sepsis risk earlier, but it does not establish that using the alert changes care in a way that improves outcomes.
The operational pilot lacks a controlled comparison, so reduced ICU transfers or mortality cannot be attributed to the model.
The evidence is limited to adult inpatient units in one health system and does not establish broader clinical or population scope.

ideal_critique: Separate earlier prediction from demonstrated treatment effect and note that lead time is not itself a patient outcome.
Point out that the pilot did not randomize, mandate action, or compare against a control unit, so outcome claims are unsupported.
Acknowledge that oversampling in training is not itself a flaw because evaluation preserved the original event rate.
ideal_defense: Concede that the strongest supported claim is earlier risk identification in similar adult inpatient settings rather than harm reduction.
Argue that the model may be operationally useful, but avoid asserting outcome improvement without intervention evidence.
Do not rely on the five-hour lead time alone as proof of reduced deterioration.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A controlled rollout comparing alerted versus non-alerted units finds no statistically reliable reduction in ICU transfer, vasopressor use, or mortality despite similar predictive accuracy.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A randomized or stepped-wedge deployment with a prespecified treatment pathway shows the alerted units have better sepsis outcomes than matched control units.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The alert improves process measures such as order-set opening or time to antibiotics, but patient outcomes are not measured or the estimates are too imprecise to attribute benefit.

must_not_claim: data_leakage_from_oversampling
class_imbalance_handling_invalidates_results

ground_truth_rationale: The central scope error is turning a predictive result into a clinical intervention claim. The case also includes a narrower external-validity issue because the evidence comes from adult inpatient settings in one system. The oversampling detail is a deliberate red herring because it was confined to training rather than evaluation.
notes: Easy case with explicit prediction-versus-intervention conflation. Includes a valid but potentially suspicious class-imbalance handling detail.

task_prompt:
A recruiting software vendor is presenting results for a resume-screening model intended to assign a talent-fit score before a recruiter decides whom to interview. The model was trained on historical screening outcomes for English-language applications to U.S.-based software engineering roles at one large technology company. Evaluation used applicants from the following two quarters at the same company, and the vendor reports consistent precision-recall performance across those later cohorts. The vendor also reports a fairness analysis for self-reported male and female applicants in the U.S. sample and notes that names, explicit age fields, and gender fields were excluded from training.

The sales team now wants to market the system as a robust talent-fit scorer across roles and regions, including sales hiring in Latin America and customer support hiring in Europe. Internally, some stakeholders view the broader wording as reasonable because the model uses fairly general resume signals such as job titles, years of experience, certifications, and employer history. Others point out that the labels were recruiter decisions at one firm, for one family of jobs, in one language, and under one labor market context. The vendor argues that feature standardization through an external employer-and-school taxonomy should help portability, and that the time-split evaluation is stronger than a random split. The debate is whether the current evidence is enough to support the broader product claim or whether the claim goes beyond what the experiment actually established.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claim is too broad because the evidence supports performance only for English-language U.S. software-engineering screening decisions at one company, not for other roles, regions, or hiring contexts.

planted_issues.description: Results from one company’s U.S. software-engineering pipeline do not establish validity for different job families or regions.
The target is historical recruiter advancement decisions at one firm, so the model learns that organization’s screening policy rather than a general talent construct.
Training and evaluation on English-language resumes do not establish performance for multilingual hiring contexts.
A fairness analysis on a narrow U.S. subgroup set does not justify broad fairness or portability claims elsewhere.

ideal_critique: Notice that the strongest problem is the jump from one firm’s recruiter labels to a general talent-fit claim.
Separate evidence for later quarters at the same company from evidence across roles, languages, and geographies.
Do not confuse feature removal or a narrow fairness check with proof of broad external validity.
ideal_defense: Concede that the model may be useful as a company-specific screening aid for similar roles, but not as a validated cross-region talent scorer.
Argue that time-split validation and taxonomy normalization are sensible engineering choices without claiming they solve the scope problem.
Avoid claiming universal fairness from the limited subgroup analysis.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Independent tests on non-engineering roles, non-U.S. regions, or non-English resumes show materially worse calibration or ranking quality than on the original U.S. software-engineering setting.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Prospective external evaluations across the proposed roles, languages, and regions show comparable ranking performance and subgroup behavior to the original setting.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model transfers well to some closely related engineering roles but evidence remains mixed or missing for different functions and geographies.

must_not_claim: direct_data_leakage_from_taxonomy_lookup
protected_attribute_removal_guarantees_bias

ground_truth_rationale: The scope problem is direct: a narrow organizational screening label is being marketed as a general talent score across new roles and regions. The fairness and feature-removal details are intentionally plausible but do not rescue the unsupported generalization. The temporal holdout is also a valid design choice rather than a flaw.
notes: Easy case with obvious job-family and regional overgeneralization. Includes valid but potentially distracting details about temporal validation and protected-feature exclusion.

task_prompt:
A software company has trained a language model classifier to flag potentially toxic messages in English-language public social media posts. The model was fine-tuned on a large moderation dataset labeled for abusive or hateful content and reached strong F1 on a held-out test set drawn from the same social platforms. The company now wants to integrate the classifier into enterprise customer-support software and market it as a system that can detect harmful intent in live support chats and reduce agent harassment. Product managers note that support chats are also text conversations and that the model can already identify profanity, slurs, and insults in the public-post benchmark.

The internal review group is less convinced. They note that the benchmark labels concern observable toxicity in public posts, whereas customer-support exchanges involve a different communication setting, more indirect frustration, more domain-specific jargon, and a different moderation objective. In addition, the company’s rollout plan is to surface warning banners or route chats to supervisors when the model score is high, but the proposed materials discuss reducing harassment even though no intervention study has been run. To make the case more persuasive, the product team points out that support agents manually reviewed 200 flagged chats from one beta customer and said many looked genuinely hostile. There was no blinded evaluation on a representative support-chat sample and no controlled test of whether warnings or routing changed agent outcomes. The question is whether the current evidence supports the proposed claim as written, or whether there is a more specific mismatch between what the experiment establishes and what the product messaging implies.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claim exceeds the evidence because the model was validated for toxicity detection in public social posts, not for harmful intent in support chats or for reducing agent harassment through intervention.

planted_issues.description: Validation on public social-media posts does not establish performance in enterprise customer-support chats with different language and goals.
The benchmark labels observable toxic content rather than the broader construct of harmful intent claimed in the product messaging.
No controlled evidence shows that acting on the model score reduces agent harassment or improves agent outcomes.

ideal_critique: Separate the domain shift problem from the construct mismatch between toxic text and harmful intent.
Point out that a small manual review of flagged chats is not the same as representative validation on support data.
Emphasize that outcome improvement for agents is an intervention claim that has not been tested.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current evidence may support a narrower claim like flagging potentially abusive language patterns in English support chats for review, pending proper evaluation.
Argue that the beta-chat review is at best exploratory evidence, not proof of broad deployment validity or harassment reduction.
Avoid claiming the model measures intent when the labels do not directly support that construct.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Representative support-chat evaluation shows lower detection quality than on public posts or warnings fail to reduce agent-reported harassment in a controlled rollout.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A prespecified validation on support-chat corpora plus a controlled intervention study shows reliable chat-domain performance and measurable reductions in agent harassment outcomes.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The classifier transfers moderately well to support chats but no intervention study measures whether routing or warnings improve agent outcomes.

must_not_claim: manual_review_sample_proves_data_leakage
use_of_pretrained_language_model_is_inherently_invalid

ground_truth_rationale: The product wording contains two distinct scope errors: transfer from public social posts to support chats and transfer from a detection benchmark to an intervention claim about reduced harassment. The beta review detail is deliberately weak but not itself a planted methodological flaw. A good answer should keep the critique focused on the specific unsupported claims.
notes: Medium case with prediction-versus-intervention conflation plus domain and construct shift. Added empirical_test_agreed as secondary resolution: the generalization claim could be tested empirically. Relabeled from medium to easy: single-pass assessor expected to score ≥0.85 mean.

task_prompt: A retail bank is evaluating a binary classifier that routes card transactions either to auto-approval or to a manual fraud review queue. The new model is compared against a rule-based system on an untouched three-month holdout containing 180,000 transactions. The team reports only recall, false-positive rate, and expected dollar loss at a decision threshold of 0.12, which initially looks unusually low for a fraud model. In the protocol document written before model training, however, the threshold was fixed from a cost matrix supplied by the fraud operations group: a missed fraud event was estimated to cost about 18 times as much as an unnecessary review, and the review team could absorb any policy that sent at most 2.5% of transactions to manual review. The stated objective was not to maximize generic classification accuracy or AUROC; it was to minimize expected fraud loss under the existing review-capacity constraint. The team did not sweep thresholds on the holdout, and all threshold selection was done on an earlier development split using the pre-specified cost matrix. On the final holdout, the new model sends 2.3% of transactions to review, improves recall relative to the rules engine, and reduces expected loss under the same budget. The report also notes that the threshold would be inappropriate for a different bank with different fraud prevalence or review costs. A critic reading the summary may still object that 0.12 is suspiciously low and must have been chosen to make recall look better, but the writeup explicitly frames the evaluation around the operational loss function and fixed queue size.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the low threshold was fixed from a pre-specified cost matrix for a constrained review workflow and validated on an untouched holdout.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Confirm from the experiment log that threshold 0.12 was locked before examining the holdout, then verify on the untouched holdout that review rate stays at or below 2.5% and expected dollar loss is lower than the rule-based baseline.

planted_issues.description: A critic may argue that the unusually low decision threshold was chosen post hoc to inflate recall, but that concern is invalid because the threshold was fixed from a pre-specified cost matrix and the claim is explicitly about expected loss under a review-capacity constraint.

ideal_critique: Check whether the threshold and cost matrix were specified before the holdout was examined and whether the reported review volume respects the operational budget.
A calibrated critique can note that the result may not transfer to institutions with different fraud prevalence or review costs, but that is a scope limitation rather than a flaw in this evaluation.
ideal_defense: Explain that the estimand is expected fraud loss under a fixed review queue, not maximum global accuracy, and that the threshold was chosen before final evaluation.
Point to the untouched holdout and the fact that the reported review rate remains within the pre-declared 2.5% capacity limit.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Audit logs show that threshold 0.12 was tuned on the holdout or that the reported policy exceeds the stated 2.5% manual-review budget.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Versioned experiment records show threshold 0.12 came from the pre-specified cost matrix before holdout access, and the untouched holdout shows lower expected dollar loss than baseline while review volume stays within budget.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The threshold was nominally pre-specified but documentation is incomplete, or holdout loss improves only within uncertainty while budget compliance is clear.

must_not_claim: The low threshold alone proves threshold tuning on the test set.
A fraud model is invalid unless it optimizes AUROC rather than the pre-specified loss function.
Any false-positive rate above the baseline is automatically a methodological flaw even when expected loss under the fixed review budget improves.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Very low decision threshold; emphasis on recall and expected loss instead of generic accuracy metrics; higher false-positive rate than baseline but still within a pre-specified manual-review budget.
ground_truth_rationale: The threshold choice is unusual but justified by the bank's pre-specified cost matrix and review-capacity constraint. Because the claim is framed around expected loss under that workflow and validated on an untouched holdout, the setup is methodologically sound.
notes: Easy case anchored on a pre-specified cost-sensitive threshold that superficially looks like post hoc optimization.

task_prompt: A hospital analytics group is evaluating a model that prioritizes chest X-rays for radiologist review. Their external validation report focuses on non-inferiority to the current technician-led priority system rather than superiority. The study uses 620 consecutively collected exams from a partner hospital, which some readers may view as too small for a medical ML validation. The protocol, however, was written before data collection and specified a non-inferiority margin of 0.03 AUROC units based on what the radiology department considered clinically negligible for queue ordering. A power analysis in that same protocol concluded that roughly 600 cases would provide 80% power to rule out performance worse than the current workflow by more than that margin, assuming the partner site's disease prevalence and label noise observed in a pilot month. After lock, the team collected 620 cases, evaluated the frozen model once, and reported a bootstrap confidence interval for the AUROC difference between the model-assisted queue and the existing process. The lower bound stayed above the negative 0.03 margin, so the study supports non-inferiority but not superiority. The report explicitly says the sample is too small for strong subgroup claims and only supports use in hospitals with similar imaging hardware and labeling policy. Because the headline table shows a modest point estimate and a cohort under 1,000 exams, a critic might say the validation is underpowered and therefore uninterpretable, but the design was built around a non-inferiority question with a pre-specified margin and a formal sample-size justification.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the apparently small cohort was sized by a pre-specified non-inferiority power analysis and the claim is limited to ruling out a clinically meaningful drop in performance.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Verify that the protocol specified the 0.03 non-inferiority margin and target sample size before data collection, then confirm that the frozen-model AUROC difference confidence interval excludes worse-than-margin performance on the external cohort.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say the external cohort is too small to support any conclusion, but that is invalid here because the study was explicitly powered for a non-inferiority margin rather than for detecting superiority or fine-grained subgroup effects.

ideal_critique: Check whether the non-inferiority margin and sample-size calculation were fixed prospectively and whether the reported conclusion stays within that narrower claim.
A careful critique may note that subgroup and cross-hospital generalization remain limited, but those limitations do not negate the stated non-inferiority result.
ideal_defense: Clarify that the question was non-inferiority to the current triage workflow, not broad superiority, and that the study was powered accordingly.
Point to the pre-registered margin, prospective sample-size calculation, and single frozen-model evaluation on consecutively collected external cases.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The non-inferiority margin or sample-size target was chosen after examining results, or the confidence interval crosses the pre-specified negative 0.03 margin.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The protocol shows a prospective non-inferiority margin and power analysis, and the external-cohort confidence interval excludes performance worse than the allowed margin.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The point estimate favors the model but the confidence interval barely overlaps the non-inferiority margin or documentation of the prospective design is incomplete.

must_not_claim: Any medical ML validation with fewer than 1,000 cases is automatically underpowered.
Failure to prove superiority means the model failed validation.
Limited subgroup power invalidates the main non-inferiority claim.

suspicious_but_valid_features: External validation sample under 1,000 cases; confidence interval emphasized more than the point estimate; claim restricted to non-inferiority rather than superiority; subgroup analyses intentionally not overinterpreted.
ground_truth_rationale: The sample size looks small only if one assumes the wrong estimand. For a prospectively defined non-inferiority question with a clinically chosen margin, the powered external validation is appropriate and the narrower scope is explicitly stated.
notes: Easy case centered on a pre-specified power analysis and non-inferiority framing.

task_prompt: A subscription music service is testing a next-item recommendation model for the homepage shown to existing paying users. The evaluation uses a random interaction split within a 14-day window instead of a chronological split, which can look suspicious because recommendation work often worries about temporal leakage. The team anticipated that objection and included a pre-analysis section before the benchmark numbers. Using three earlier quarters of logs, they compared model rankings under random interaction splits and under week-forward chronological splits for the same stable subscriber population. Across those historical windows, the ranking of candidate models changed minimally and NDCG differed by less than 0.3 points on average. They also documented that 99.5% of evaluation users were already active subscribers before the window began, catalog turnover during the two-week period was negligible, and the deployment target is short-horizon reranking for the same mature user base rather than cold-start recommendation for new users or long-range demand forecasting. Based on that empirical stability analysis, they specified random splitting for the main benchmark because it allowed more efficient use of sparse positive interactions without changing model ordering in this deployment setting. The final report still notes that the results should not be reused to make claims about new-user generalization or rapidly changing catalogs. A critic could nevertheless see the random split and infer leakage or an unrealistically easy test set, but the writeup expressly ties that split choice to a documented i.i.d.-like regime and to the narrow production use case being claimed.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the random split was justified by empirical pre-analysis showing negligible difference from chronological evaluation for this stable short-horizon reranking setting.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Replicate the historical comparison between random and chronological splits on earlier windows and verify that model ordering and NDCG differences remain negligible for the existing-subscriber population targeted by deployment.

planted_issues.description: A critic may treat any random split in recommendation evaluation as temporal leakage, but that concern is invalid here because pre-analysis showed random and chronological splits behave equivalently for the stable existing-user setting and the claim is explicitly limited to that setting.

ideal_critique: Ask whether the empirical split-comparison really covers the intended deployment population and whether the report avoids extending the result to cold-start users or fast-changing catalogs.
A good critique can flag that random splitting would be inappropriate for broader recommendation claims, while still accepting that it may be valid for this short-horizon stable-user benchmark.
ideal_defense: Point to the historical pre-analysis showing negligible metric and model-ordering differences between random and chronological evaluation in the targeted regime.
Emphasize that the deployment claim is narrow: reranking for existing subscribers over a short window with minimal catalog churn.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Historical backtests show materially different model ordering or much worse chronological performance, or the report generalizes the result to cold-start users or long-horizon recommendation.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Across prior windows, random and chronological splits produce nearly identical model ordering and small metric differences for the stable subscriber population, and the stated claim remains restricted to that population.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Split differences are small for most windows but not all, or the deployment population partly includes new users not represented in the stability analysis.

must_not_claim: Any random split in recommendation evaluation automatically invalidates the benchmark.
The model must be leaking future interactions because chronological splitting was not used.
A method is unsound unless it is evaluated for new-user cold start, even when the claim is limited to existing users.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Random interaction split in a recommendation benchmark; short 14-day evaluation window; heavy emphasis on stable existing subscribers rather than new users; explicit narrowing of claims to near-term reranking.
ground_truth_rationale: Random splits are often problematic in recommendation tasks, but not universally so. Here the team justified the choice with historical equivalence checks and limited the claim to the specific deployment regime where those checks apply.
notes: Easy empirical-pre-analysis case where random split looks suspect but is supported by prior backtests.

task_prompt:
An online marketplace is deciding whether to let a new fraud model automatically place certain orders on hold before shipment instead of sending them to the existing analyst queue. The payments team reports that the model achieved an AUC of 0.93 on six months of transaction history and says it captures fraud patterns missed by current rules. The proposed production use is narrow at first: auto-hold orders above a risk threshold for high-value electronics and gift cards during the holiday season, when analyst capacity is tight.

For development, the team labeled fraud using confirmed chargebacks and account takeovers that were resolved within 90 days. Transactions that were already blocked by current rules were excluded because they never reached fulfillment. Approved historical orders were scored retrospectively, and the model's ranking performance looked strong across card brands and regions. The presentation emphasizes that the positive class is rare and that a low false positive rate at the chosen threshold would still reduce analyst workload meaningfully.

Operations leaders ask whether the backtest is sufficient evidence for this production change, given that auto-holds would delay some legitimate orders. They also note that many holiday transactions do not have final dispute outcomes for several weeks, so the team is using the most recent fully matured period. Consider whether the evaluation supports the deployment decision, not just whether the model separates known fraud from known non-fraud in hindsight.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The backtest does not adequately justify auto-holding orders because the labels and sample omit key cases and the operational cost of false holds is underexamined.

planted_issues.description: Excluding transactions already blocked by existing rules changes the evaluated population relative to the proposed live queue and may distort threshold performance.
Using only matured chargebacks means the evaluation may miss unresolved recent fraud and overstate certainty about prospective holiday performance.
In deployment, false positives delay legitimate orders and can cause abandonment or customer churn, which is not captured by AUC.

ideal_critique: Connect the retrospective ranking result to the actual deployment choice, which is an automatic hold threshold on a live order stream.
Note that excluding transactions already intercepted by current controls can make the evaluated slice unlike the production slice.
Emphasize that false holds have asymmetric commercial costs, especially during holiday delivery windows.
ideal_defense: Acknowledge that mature-label periods are reasonable for training and basic model development.
Concede that a live or shadow test is needed before replacing human review with automatic holds.
Argue that the model may still be useful for prioritization even if full automation is premature.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a shadow deployment on current holiday traffic, the chosen threshold would place more than 2% of legitimate high-value orders on hold or show materially worse precision than the retrospective estimate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a shadow deployment with matured follow-up, the chosen threshold cuts analyst workload by at least 25% while keeping legitimate-order hold rate below 0.5% and fraud capture within 5 percentage points of current controls.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Shadow results show lower workload but fraud outcomes remain partially immature or legitimate-order delay costs are not quantified.

must_not_claim: Using the latest fully matured period is inherently a mistake.
Rare-class imbalance alone proves the model is unusable.

ground_truth_rationale: This is not just a fraud-detection research claim; it is a decision to automatically stop orders. Because labels are delayed and the retrospective slice differs from the proposed live slice, the team lacks reliable evidence on real hold rates and customer harm.
notes: Easy case. Holiday fraud domain. Mature-label period is a valid feature that may look suspicious but is reasonable.

task_prompt:
A payments company is reviewing a new fraud-ranking model for card-not-present transactions. The legacy baseline is a gradient boosted tree trained on historical authorizations, and the new model is a larger ensemble with merchant and device velocity features. In the quarterly review deck, the baseline precision-recall AUC is listed as 0.214 based on a Q1 evaluation, while the challenger is reported at 0.220 on a Q2 evaluation. The team frames the six-basis-point gain as enough to justify migration because even small improvements affect investigation workload.

The documentation explains that the Q1 baseline score was produced from the standard dashboard, which uses provisional fraud labels available 30 days after authorization. For the challenger, the team waited for a 90-day maturity window because a newer dispute feed is now available and they wanted more complete labels before signing off. They also note that fraud prevalence remains around two-tenths of one percent, that both models were trained with forward-looking temporal splits by week rather than random splits, and that the Q2 evaluation covers a period after a merchant onboarding campaign changed the mix of transaction sizes and geographies. No confidence intervals or paired significance analysis are included in the deck because the absolute gain looks operationally meaningful to the fraud operations lead.

Management wants to treat the PR-AUC difference as evidence that the new model is better. Review whether the benchmark comparison is sound and what evidence would be required to support the migration decision.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claimed gain is not benchmark-valid because the models were compared on different quarters with different label maturation, and the tiny delta is reported without uncertainty.

planted_issues.description: The baseline uses 30-day provisional fraud labels while the challenger uses 90-day matured labels, so the target definition differs across evaluations.
The baseline and challenger are evaluated on different quarters with different transaction mix, confounding model quality with population shift.
A six-basis-point PR-AUC gain is presented without confidence intervals or a paired test, so the reported improvement may be noise.

ideal_critique: Notice that fraud labels mature over time, so 30-day and 90-day outcomes are not equivalent targets.
Point out that Q1 and Q2 transaction populations differ, making the side-by-side comparison non-causal.
Ask for paired uncertainty estimates because the claimed gain is small in absolute terms.
ideal_defense: Concede that temporal splits are sensible for fraud and are not the main problem here.
Argue only for a rerun on a common quarter with a common maturity horizon, not for accepting the current deck at face value.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: When both models are rescored on the same quarter using the same 90-day matured labels, the PR-AUC gap is under 0.003 or the paired bootstrap interval crosses zero.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On the same quarter with the same 90-day matured labels, the challenger exceeds the baseline by at least 0.006 PR-AUC and the paired bootstrap 95% interval excludes zero.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A common-quarter rerun shows a positive gap above zero but below 0.006 PR-AUC and the interval barely overlaps zero.

must_not_claim: Using temporal splits by week is invalid because the split is not randomized.
PR-AUC is automatically an invalid metric for rare-event fraud.

ground_truth_rationale: The important flaw is not the temporal split; that is often the right design in fraud. The benchmark is broken because the evaluation windows and label maturity horizons differ, and the deck omits uncertainty despite a very small reported gain.
notes: Medium case with a valid-looking temporal split as a red herring. Reviewers should focus on label maturation and quarter mismatch, not on rarity of the positive class. Added empirical_test_agreed as secondary resolution: the baseline flaw could be confirmed by re-running with corrected evaluation.

task_prompt:
An insurance analytics group is benchmarking an AutoML system for classifying whether a claim will require special investigation. The baseline is a manually tuned logistic regression with one-hot categorical features and standard missing-value handling. The challenger is an AutoML search over gradient boosting and linear pipelines with target encoding for several high-cardinality fields such as repair shop, policy channel, and assessor ID. The final slide reports a clear win for the AutoML approach on ROC-AUC and a larger lift at the operating threshold used by the investigations team.

The methods appendix says the team first assembled a single modeling table from two years of claims and then applied median imputation and target encoding to the full table before creating cross-validation folds for the AutoML search. After selecting the best pipeline, they evaluated it on a held-out test set and chose the score cutoff that achieved 80% recall on that same test set because the investigations queue has a fixed service-level target. The logistic baseline was tuned earlier with nested cross-validation and continues to use the threshold selected from validation data in the original project. The report does not show a rerun of the logistic model under the AutoML preprocessing pipeline because the team considered the newer feature engineering part of the improved system.

A steering committee wants to accept the benchmark and move to implementation. Assess whether the evaluation design fairly demonstrates that AutoML beat the baseline and what comparison would be needed to justify that claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The benchmark is invalid because preprocessing leaked information across folds and the final threshold was tuned on the test set under an asymmetric protocol.

planted_issues.description: Median imputation and target encoding were fit on the full modeling table before fold creation, leaking label and distribution information into evaluation.
The final operating threshold was chosen on the held-out test set, contaminating the reported queue-level lift.
The challenger benefits from a newer preprocessing and threshold-selection protocol while the baseline remains under its older validation-only setup, so the comparison is not balanced.

ideal_critique: Spot that target encoding and imputation were applied before fold creation, which is a classic leakage path.
Notice that selecting the recall threshold on the test set turns the benchmark into model selection.
Recommend rebuilding both pipelines inside train-fold-only preprocessing and choosing thresholds on validation data for both.
ideal_defense: Concede that target encoding can be valid if cross-fitted; the problem is not the technique itself but how it was applied here.
Argue only that the AutoML system may still win after a leakage-free rerun, not that the current numbers prove it.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After all preprocessing is fit inside training folds and thresholds are selected on validation data, the AutoML lift at 80% recall shrinks below 1 percentage point or reverses.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: With leakage-free fold-specific preprocessing and validation-only threshold selection for both systems, the AutoML pipeline still improves ROC-AUC by at least 0.02 and precision at 80% recall by at least 3 points.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A fair rerun preserves the ROC-AUC gain but not the queue-level threshold gain, or vice versa.

must_not_claim: Target encoding is always leakage even when cross-fitted within training folds.
AutoML is inherently unfair to compare against hand-tuned models.

ground_truth_rationale: Two separate protocol problems contaminate the benchmark: full-data preprocessing before splitting and test-set threshold choice. The case also checks whether the reviewer can distinguish target encoding as a technique from target encoding applied incorrectly.
notes: Medium case. Three must-find issues. Good for testing whether a model can identify leakage in preprocessing without overclaiming that all target encoding is invalid.

task_prompt:
A large company is considering a model to rank internal applicants for promotion interviews. The analytics team reports that the new model improves macro-F1 from 0.71 to 0.78 on historical promotion decisions and writes in the proposal that the system is "fairer across gender groups" than the manual process currently used. The training labels are prior manager recommendations that were later approved or rejected by a committee. The team says names and photos were removed from the feature set, job level was standardized across departments, and the test split was taken from a later time period rather than random rows.

The evaluation packet focuses on overall macro-F1 and a single confusion matrix for the full employee population. It does not show false-positive, false-negative, calibration, or ranking quality broken out by gender or by intersectional subgroup. It also does not specify what notion of fairness the proposal is using: equal opportunity, calibration, demographic parity, or something else. Some executives argue that if predictive performance on historical decisions improved after removing explicit identity fields, then the process is probably fairer in practice. Others are uneasy because the claim being made is specifically about fairness, not just accuracy relative to past decisions.

Your task is to assess whether the evidence supports the fairness claim. Focus on whether the metric reported actually measures the property named in the proposal, and whether any reasonable defense of the current evaluation exists. You may discuss what additional analysis would be needed before the company could responsibly make the stated claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Improved overall macro-F1 on past promotion decisions does not establish that the model is fairer across gender groups without explicit fairness metric evaluation.

planted_issues.description: Macro-F1 measures predictive performance, not fairness, so it cannot directly support a fairness claim.
No subgroup error, calibration, or ranking analysis is shown for the groups named in the claim.
Matching historical promotion recommendations more closely does not show fairness if the historical labels may themselves reflect bias or a different objective.

ideal_critique: State that fairness is not a synonym for better overall prediction on historical labels.
Ask the team to specify the fairness definition and evaluate it by subgroup.
Note that removing names or using a temporal split are good practices but do not validate the fairness claim.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current packet does not support the fairness language as written.
Argue only that the model may be a candidate for further fairness analysis because explicit identifiers were removed and performance improved.
Avoid implying that improved macro-F1 against historical labels proves a fairer process.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Once a prespecified fairness criterion is evaluated, the new model fails to improve or worsens group disparities despite the higher macro-F1.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Under a prespecified fairness criterion, the new model improves subgroup outcomes or disparities relative to the manual process while maintaining acceptable utility.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves one fairness criterion but worsens another, leaving the company to choose among incompatible definitions.

must_not_claim: removing_names_is_bad_practice
temporal_holdout_is_invalid

ground_truth_rationale: The proposal makes a fairness claim, but the only quantitative evidence is overall macro-F1 on historical decisions. Without a defined fairness notion and subgroup evaluation, the claim is unsupported, and historical-label fit may even track prior bias.
notes: Medium HR fairness case. Name removal and temporal splitting are deliberate red herrings that are valid practices, not planted flaws. Upgraded to mixed: the fairness question is genuinely empirical and depends on criterion choice. Reverted to critique: the prompt provides no fairness definition or subgroup evidence, making empirical_test_agreed the secondary resolution rather than primary.

task_prompt:
An education company is reviewing an adaptive tutoring policy for middle-school math. The reinforcement-learning team reports that the new policy raises offline expected reward in historical replay and increases immediate next-problem accuracy by 8 percentage points in a simulator built from prior student sessions. The launch memo says the policy will "improve student learning gains by the end of the term." The simulator reward heavily weights whether a student answers the next problem correctly after receiving a hint or worked example.

The company has rich session logs but only sparse delayed assessments, because most classrooms administer common post-tests at the end of units rather than after every lesson. Teachers also vary in how they override recommendations, and some students stop using the product when exercises feel too easy or too hard. The evaluation packet does not report delayed retention, transfer to novel problems, or end-of-unit assessment outcomes. It also does not show whether the replay estimator remains reliable when the new policy would send students into content states that were uncommon under the old policy. The team argues that immediate correctness is the most practical proxy available and that the simulator reproduces historical clickstream statistics well.

Your task is to assess whether the current evidence supports the launch memo's claim. Focus on whether the reported metrics align with the outcome named in the memo and whether the offline evidence is strong enough to justify that statement. You may discuss both what is persuasive in the current packet and what remains unresolved.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Simulator reward and immediate next-problem accuracy do not adequately support a claim about end-of-term learning gains.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a classroom or student-level randomized study measuring delayed post-test or end-of-unit assessment gains, along with engagement and dropout effects under the new policy.

planted_issues.description: Immediate next-problem correctness is an imperfect and potentially misleading proxy for long-term learning gains or transfer.
Offline replay and simulation may be unreliable if the new policy induces student-content trajectories that were rare under the logging policy.
End-of-term learning can be affected by dropout, frustration, and teacher overrides that are not captured by the reported immediate reward metric.

ideal_critique: Separate short-run task success from the stronger educational claim about durable learning and transfer.
Question whether the offline evaluator is valid under policy shift when the tutor may visit underrepresented states.
Ask for delayed assessment outcomes and engagement effects, not just simulator reward.
ideal_defense: Concede that the memo language is too strong for the current evidence.
Argue that immediate correctness and a well-fitting simulator can still justify piloting or experimentation when delayed labels are sparse.
Avoid claiming that the practical difficulty of collecting delayed outcomes makes the existing proxy sufficient.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Randomized classroom evaluation finds no improvement in delayed assessments or shows worse engagement or dropout despite higher immediate accuracy.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Randomized evaluation shows statistically significant end-of-unit or end-of-term learning gains with stable or improved engagement under the new policy.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Immediate accuracy and some unit assessments improve, but effects vary sharply by teacher intervention level or by student subgroup.

must_not_claim: simulators_are_always_invalid
sparse_delayed_assessments_make_evaluation_impossible

ground_truth_rationale: The memo's target outcome is long-term learning, but the reported metric is immediate correctness under an offline simulator. In tutoring systems, delayed retention, transfer, engagement, and policy-shift validity all matter, so the evidence does not support the stronger claim without randomized educational outcome measurement.
notes: Hard education/RL case. Designed to test recognition that practical proxies can still be the wrong metric for the stated claim. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt:
A payments company is considering a new fraud model for card-not-present transactions. During a six-week pilot, the model was enabled only for merchants that volunteered for an early access program. Those merchants received model scores and could configure their own approval thresholds, while the rest of the network continued on the legacy rules engine. The results deck highlights that pilot merchants saw a lower fraud-loss rate and fewer customer complaints about false declines than merchants that stayed on the old system.

The deck says the comparison used the same calendar weeks for both groups and that labels came from chargebacks and confirmed fraud investigations. Pilot merchants were mostly large retailers with dedicated risk teams, because they were the ones willing to participate early and integrate the score feed quickly. The company also notes that some pilot merchants had recently adopted stronger checkout authentication features, but this is discussed only as implementation context rather than as part of the model evaluation. The appendix further states that detailed labels are only available for approved transactions, since declined transactions generally do not produce downstream fraud outcomes.

The proposal recommends expanding the model network-wide, arguing that the pilot demonstrates both lower fraud and fewer false positives in real production traffic.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The pilot does not isolate model impact because merchant selection, concurrent checkout changes, and selective labels can all explain the observed differences.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a randomized or switchback test within comparable merchants while tracking consistent fraud and approval outcomes, including a design that accounts for unlabeled declined transactions.

planted_issues.description: Early-access merchants volunteered and were disproportionately large, sophisticated retailers, so their baseline fraud patterns may differ from the rest of the network.
Some pilot merchants also changed checkout authentication during the same period, creating a competing explanation for lower fraud losses.
Fraud labels are observed mainly for approved transactions, so comparing false-positive and fraud outcomes across different approval policies can be misleading.

ideal_critique: Note that pilot merchants were not randomly chosen and likely differ systematically from non-pilot merchants.
Identify the checkout authentication rollout as a treatment-period interaction that can reduce fraud independently of the model.
Explain that approved-only labels make cross-group comparisons sensitive to differing decline policies.
ideal_defense: Concede that the network-wide causal claim is too strong on the current evidence.
Argue only that the pilot is promising enough to justify a cleaner within-merchant experiment.
Avoid claiming that same-calendar-week comparison by itself removes the main confounds.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Within comparable merchants or randomized traffic slices, the apparent fraud and false-decline advantage shrinks materially after controlling for authentication changes and approval-rate shifts.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A within-merchant randomized test shows the model lowers fraud losses without raising false declines after holding authentication settings fixed.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Fraud improves on some merchants but the effect size varies with changing approval rates and concurrent checkout settings.

must_not_claim: using device or IP features is automatically invalid
class imbalance alone explains the whole result

ground_truth_rationale: The company is comparing unlike merchants during a period when some of the pilot merchants also changed security controls. On top of that, outcomes are observed differently depending on which transactions were approved, so the pilot result is not a clean model effect.
notes: Medium case with interacting self-selection and label-observation issues in fraud detection.

task_prompt:
A hospital quality team is evaluating a sepsis risk model intended to prioritize rapid review by a response nurse. The model went live on November 1 in two emergency departments. In a summary to leadership, the team reports that mortality among patients flagged by the model in November and December was lower than mortality among a historical comparison group from July through September, and they present this as evidence that the model is improving care.

The summary explains that the alert itself did not prescribe treatment, but flagged patients were discussed more quickly once the model was active. Around the same time, the hospital opened a winter surge unit and updated its empiric antibiotic guidance for suspected sepsis. The inclusion criteria for the analysis were adults admitted through the emergency department who had a lactate test ordered within the first few hours, because that was the easiest subgroup to extract reliably from the electronic record. The writeup does not include a concurrent control unit or a comparison to the same months in the prior year. It also does not separate whether mortality changes reflected the model, the operational changes, or differences in seasonal patient mix.

The model uses standard bedside vitals and lab values and was internally validated before deployment. Leadership is deciding whether to extend it across the rest of the hospital.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The mortality comparison is confounded by seasonal and operational changes, so it does not show that the model caused better outcomes.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Use a concurrent control group or stepped-wedge rollout, and compare the same seasonal window while accounting for protocol and unit changes.

planted_issues.description: Comparing November-December patients with July-September patients mixes the model effect with seasonal differences in emergency-department case mix and acuity.
The winter surge unit and updated antibiotic guidance changed care processes during the same period as model deployment.
Restricting analysis to patients who had early lactate testing can select a changing subgroup whose composition depends on clinician behavior.

ideal_critique: Recognize that the comparison crosses very different clinical periods without a concurrent control.
Point out that multiple care-process changes happened at the same time as the model launch.
Note that inclusion depends on lactate ordering, which may itself change after the model or protocol rollout.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current evidence is insufficient for a causal claim about mortality reduction.
Argue only that the model may merit broader study if a controlled rollout is feasible.
Do not claim that internal validation of prediction accuracy proves downstream outcome impact.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A same-season controlled analysis shows no mortality improvement once unit changes, antibiotic guidance, and patient mix are accounted for.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A stepped-wedge or concurrent-control evaluation shows better process measures or outcomes attributable to the alert while care protocols are otherwise comparable.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Outcomes improve modestly, but the effect overlaps with protocol and seasonal shifts that cannot be cleanly separated.

must_not_claim: using bedside vitals and labs is itself leakage
internal validation on the hospital's own data automatically invalidates the model

ground_truth_rationale: Outcome improvement after deployment could reflect winter case-mix differences, operational expansions, or the new antibiotic protocol rather than the model. The analysis also conditions on lactate ordering, which can shift the patient subset being measured.
notes: Clinical temporal confounding case requiring some hospital-operations awareness.

task_prompt:
A consumer lender is reviewing a collections model that ranks delinquent accounts by expected near-term recovery. In a board update, the servicing team reports that average recovered balance per assigned account increased by 16% after the model replaced the prior queueing rules. The comparison uses accounts worked from February through April after deployment versus accounts worked from November through January before deployment. The board memo frames the change as evidence that the model is improving collection efficiency.

The later period overlaps tax-refund season, when many borrowers receive temporary liquidity and recovery patterns often change. During the same time, the lender switched to a new dialer vendor that verified more phone numbers and reduced unreachable accounts in the work queue. Managers were also allowed to pull certain hardship or recently promised-to-pay customers to the front of the queue, even if their model rank was lower, because they wanted to manage service-level commitments. The memo does not show a same-period control group, and it does not compare recovery separately for accounts newly reachable under the dialer change. It also does not explain whether agents handled the same number of accounts per shift in both periods.

The model uses standard account, payment-history, and contactability features. Executives agree the metric matters financially, but they want to know whether the observed gain should be treated as strong evidence that the model itself caused better recoveries.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The observed recovery gain is too confounded by refund-season timing, contactability changes, and queue overrides to attribute it confidently to the model.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a same-period randomized queue test or matched champion-challenger design while holding dialer reachability and manual override policies constant.

planted_issues.description: February through April collections behavior can improve for seasonal cash-flow reasons unrelated to the ranking model.
The new dialer vendor changed which accounts were reachable, altering the queue population during the same period as model rollout.
Managers could reorder accounts for hardship or promised-to-pay commitments, so treatment assignment did not follow the model alone.
