Without a contemporaneous control or champion-challenger split, before-after recovery differences are not attributable solely to the model.

ideal_critique: Spot that the evaluation crosses tax-refund season, which is a major omitted temporal factor in collections.
Notice that the new dialer changed the reachable population, which directly affects recovery rates.
Explain that manual queue overrides further break any clean mapping from model rank to treatment.
Request a same-period champion-challenger or randomized queue design.
ideal_defense: Concede that the before-after board memo is not enough for a firm causal conclusion.
Argue only that the improvement is worth testing more rigorously because a real model effect remains possible.
Do not claim that using contactability features is automatically unlawful or that recovered balance is inherently the wrong business metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A same-period champion-challenger test shows little difference once reachability and override policies are held fixed, or the gain is concentrated in newly reachable refund-season accounts.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Within the same calendar period and under stable dialer and override rules, model-ranked accounts deliver materially higher recoveries than the legacy queue.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model adds some value, but a large part of the board-level gain comes from refund-season timing and improved contactability rather than ranking quality.

must_not_claim: using contactability features is automatically unlawful
recovered balance is inherently an invalid collections metric

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case hides the main confounds in ordinary operational details: refund season, reachability changes, and manual queue management. The claim is too strong because the memo never compares the model to the old process under the same servicing conditions.
notes: Hard tabular/collections case with temporal confounding and several interacting high-severity issues. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt:
A cancer center has trained a pathology-image model to predict six-month progression-free survival for patients with early-stage breast cancer who received treatment at that center over the last decade. The model was evaluated retrospectively on held-out slides from the same institution and appeared to improve risk stratification beyond standard clinical covariates. The translational research team is enthusiastic because the model seems to capture visual patterns not represented in structured pathology reports. A draft slide for hospital leadership says the system can guide adjuvant therapy selection across solid tumors.

Several oncologists are uneasy with that wording. They note that the current label is prognosis under historical treatment patterns at one center, not a direct estimate of treatment benefit for different regimens. They also point out that the study population consists only of early-stage breast cancer cases, while the proposed claim reaches across very different tumor types with distinct biology, therapies, and pathology workflows. In addition, treatment choices in the retrospective data were not randomized; they reflected clinician judgment, patient comorbidities, and evolving standards of care over ten years. Supporters of the broader statement reply that the model is not being pitched as a fully autonomous prescribing system and that any tool improving prognosis estimation is naturally relevant to treatment planning. The debate is whether that logic is enough to justify the cross-tumor therapy-guidance claim, or whether the current evidence supports only a much narrower statement about prognostic enrichment in a specific clinical setting.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claim overreaches because the study supports prognosis prediction for early-stage breast cancer at one center, not treatment-selection guidance across solid tumors.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Multi-center studies that estimate treatment-benefit heterogeneity, not just prognosis, within each claimed tumor type and therapy context.

planted_issues.description: Predicting progression-free survival under historical care does not establish which therapy will work better for a patient.
Evidence from early-stage breast cancer does not justify claims across solid tumors with different biology and treatment pathways.
Results from one center’s pathology workflow and case mix do not establish broad clinical portability.
Historical treatment choices were confounded by clinician judgment and evolving standards, so therapy-guidance claims are unsupported.

ideal_critique: Make the key distinction between prognosis and treatment-effect estimation rather than merely saying the study is retrospective.
Call out the leap from one cancer type at one center to therapy guidance across solid tumors.
Explain why historical treatment confounding matters specifically for treatment-selection claims.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current evidence may support a narrower claim about prognostic stratification for early-stage breast cancer in similar pathology workflows.
Argue that relevance to treatment planning is not the same as validated treatment-selection guidance.
Avoid claiming that better prognosis models automatically identify the best regimen.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Follow-up studies fail to show that the model predicts differential treatment benefit within breast cancer or perform poorly when transferred to other tumor types or centers.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Prospective or carefully causal studies within each claimed tumor context show that the model identifies clinically meaningful treatment-benefit heterogeneity beyond standard covariates.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model remains prognostically useful in additional centers but no convincing evidence demonstrates regimen-selection value or cross-tumor transfer.

must_not_claim: whole_slide_imaging_is_inherently_invalid
retrospective_study_design_alone_proves_no_value

ground_truth_rationale: This case combines two scope jumps that are easy to blur together: prognosis versus treatment selection, and one cancer setting versus all solid tumors. A good critique should mention both, while a good defense should narrow the claim rather than deny the confounding and generalization problems. The slide wording is too broad even if the prognostic model itself is promising.
notes: Hard critique case with interacting causal and clinical-generalization issues in oncology. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt: A customer-support team is evaluating an NLP classifier that either sends a templated auto-reply or defers a ticket to a human agent. The paper reports precision, harmful-error rate, and coverage only for tickets above a confidence threshold of 0.92, while all lower-confidence tickets are routed to humans and excluded from the auto-reply error metric. That can look like cherry-picking because the model is not being scored on every incoming ticket. The threshold, however, was written into a pre-registration before model development because legal and operations stakeholders defined a system-level requirement: wrong automated replies had to stay below 1% of auto-resolved tickets, and at least 30% of total ticket volume needed to be eligible for automation to justify launch. The model was then trained, calibrated on a development split, and frozen. On the untouched test set, 37% of tickets exceeded the threshold, the harmful-error rate among auto-resolved tickets was 0.7%, and the remainder were intentionally sent to human agents. The report also provides the abstention rate and states clearly that the claim is about the full selective-prediction system, not about universal classification over all tickets. The authors note that a different support organization with a different tolerance for automation errors would need a different threshold. A critic might still object that excluding abstained cases makes the reported precision artificially high, but the whole product decision is whether the abstaining system safely automates a subset while leaving the rest to people, and that operating point was fixed before the final test.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the confidence threshold and selective-prediction objective were pre-specified, and the evaluation correctly measures the deployed abstaining system rather than a universal classifier.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Verify that the 0.92 threshold and coverage/error targets were registered before the test set was used, then confirm on the untouched test set that auto-resolution coverage is at least 30% and harmful-error rate among auto-resolved tickets is below 1%.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say the model inflates precision by excluding hard cases, but that concern is invalid because the deployed system is explicitly an abstaining classifier with a pre-registered threshold and separately reported coverage.

ideal_critique: Check whether the threshold and safety targets were set prospectively and whether coverage is reported alongside error so the selective system can be evaluated honestly.
A calibrated critique may note that results do not imply high-quality performance on abstained tickets, but that is not the claim being made.
ideal_defense: Explain that the product is a selective-prediction pipeline that intentionally abstains on uncertain cases, so evaluating auto-resolved tickets plus coverage is the correct system-level metric.
Point to the pre-registered 1% harmful-error ceiling, 30% minimum coverage target, and untouched test-set results at the locked threshold.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The threshold was adjusted after reviewing test outcomes, or the system fails either the pre-specified harmful-error ceiling or the minimum coverage requirement.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Documentation shows the threshold and operating targets were fixed before test evaluation, and the untouched test set meets both the harmful-error and coverage targets for the abstaining system.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Safety is comfortably met but coverage falls slightly short, or the threshold appears prospectively chosen but calibration details are incomplete.

must_not_claim: Excluding abstained tickets automatically makes the evaluation invalid.
The model must be measured as if it were forced to answer every ticket regardless of the deployed workflow.
High precision on the auto-resolved subset is evidence of test-set cherry-picking even when coverage is prospectively constrained and reported.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Metrics reported only on tickets above a high confidence threshold; a substantial abstention rate; evaluation focused on harmful-error rate and coverage rather than all-ticket accuracy; threshold chosen before model development.
ground_truth_rationale: This is a selective-prediction system, so measuring performance on accepted cases together with coverage is appropriate. Because the threshold and system targets were set in advance and tested once on untouched data, the design is sound.
notes: Medium case using a pre-registered threshold where a critic may confuse system-level abstention with cherry-picking.

task_prompt: A manufacturer is benchmarking a computer-vision defect detector for a production line where positive examples are rare. The dataset contains 9,800 product images, only 410 of which show true defects confirmed by later inspection. To address this imbalance, the training pipeline heavily oversamples positives and applies rotations, flips, and brightness changes that create many near-duplicate images. A reader skimming the methods might worry that the team effectively copied defect images into validation data and inflated performance. The pipeline description is more careful than that summary suggests. The authors use nested cross-validation, and every split is performed at the original-image level before any augmentation occurs. All augmentations are generated on the fly inside the training fold only. Hyperparameters are chosen using inner folds, the selected model is retrained on the full development set with the same fold-local augmentation policy, and it is evaluated once on a final held-out test set consisting of raw images captured during a later production week. The report also states that defect clustering by physical part number was checked so that nearly identical shots from the same physical unit do not cross split boundaries. Because the final claim is limited to this camera setup and this manufacturing process, the authors do not present the result as broad cross-factory generalization. Even so, the presence of aggressive oversampling and many synthetic positives can make the benchmark look contaminated to a critic who assumes augmentation happened before splitting, when the method section actually specifies the opposite order.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the apparently duplicative augmentation is confined to training folds after splitting, with raw later-week data reserved for final evaluation.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Check the pipeline implementation and duplicate-detection audit to confirm that augmentation occurs only after fold assignment, that no original or near-duplicate image crosses development and test boundaries, and that the final test set comes from a later production week.

planted_issues.description: A critic may think heavy oversampling and image augmentation leaked near-duplicates into evaluation, but that concern is invalid because splitting happened before augmentation and duplicate checks were performed at the original-unit level.

ideal_critique: Inspect whether split assignment truly occurred before augmentation and whether part-level grouping prevented near-identical shots of the same physical unit from crossing boundaries.
A careful critique can still note that the claim is limited to the same camera and production process, which is a disclosure issue rather than evidence of contamination.
ideal_defense: Point to the fold-local augmentation policy, nested cross-validation design, and later-week raw test set that was never augmented.
Explain that oversampling rare positives is acceptable when synthetic examples are confined to training and do not cross split boundaries.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Code or duplicate analysis shows that augmented or near-duplicate images from the same original unit appear in validation or test splits, or that augmentation was applied before splitting.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Pipeline logs confirm split assignment precedes augmentation, duplicate checks find no cross-boundary originals or near-duplicates, and the final evaluation uses raw later-week images only.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: No literal duplicates cross splits but the part-level grouping is imperfect or incompletely documented, leaving some uncertainty about near-duplicate leakage.

must_not_claim: Any use of oversampling or augmentation in an imbalanced vision task is evidence of train-test contamination.
The model is invalid because there are many synthetic positives, regardless of when augmentation occurs.
A vision benchmark must prove cross-factory generalization or it is methodologically unsound.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Aggressive oversampling of rare defects; many synthetic positive images; nested cross-validation with fold-local augmentation; claim intentionally limited to one camera setup and factory process.
ground_truth_rationale: The suspicious part is the presence of many near-duplicate synthetic images, but duplication inside training folds is not leakage. Because splitting occurs before augmentation and the final test set is raw later-week data, the evaluation remains valid.
notes: Medium computer-vision case where augmentation looks like contamination but is methodologically proper.

task_prompt: A utility company is evaluating a time-series model that forecasts store-level electricity demand 15 minutes ahead to support intraday balancing. Instead of a standard last-month holdout, the team uses blocked random windows from a six-week evaluation period. That choice can appear wrong because time-series work usually emphasizes chronological backtesting. The methods section explains why they departed from that default. Before final benchmarking, the team ran a pre-analysis on the previous six months of data and found that after conditioning on temperature, weekday, holiday status, and recent lagged load, residual autocorrelation dropped to near zero beyond two hours. They also compared model rankings under rolling-origin evaluation versus blocked random windows across four historical periods and observed less than 1% difference in MAPE and no model-order reversals in the top three candidates. The operational deployment retrains the model nightly and only needs one-step-ahead forecasts for the next 15 minutes, not long-range projections across regime shifts. Based on those empirical checks, the evaluation plan specified blocked random windows to make better use of scarce extreme-weather intervals while preserving the same short-horizon forecasting target. The report explicitly says the results should not be used to claim robustness to seasonal drift or to week-ahead forecasting. A critic might still see the random windows and assume the evaluation ignored temporal dependence, but the justification is that this particular residual process is close to i.i.d. at the chosen horizon after conditioning, and that claim was checked in historical data before the final comparison.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because blocked random windows were justified by prior residual-dependence checks and backtests showing equivalence to rolling-origin evaluation for the short-horizon retrained forecasting task.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Reproduce the pre-analysis showing low residual dependence beyond two hours and confirm across historical periods that blocked-random and rolling-origin evaluation produce materially similar model rankings and short-horizon error for the nightly retrained 15-minute forecasting task.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say any non-chronological split invalidates a forecasting study, but that concern is invalid here because empirical pre-analysis showed the chosen short-horizon conditioned process behaves similarly under blocked-random and rolling-origin evaluation and the claim is limited accordingly.

ideal_critique: Check the residual-dependence analysis and the historical comparison between blocked-random and rolling-origin evaluation instead of assuming the default split is always required.
A calibrated critique may emphasize that the result does not establish robustness to seasonal or structural drift, but that limitation is already reflected in the prompt's narrow claim.
ideal_defense: Explain that the deployment horizon is only 15 minutes with nightly retraining, so the relevant question is whether blocked-random and chronological evaluation differ in that short-horizon regime.
Point to the historical backtests showing negligible ranking changes and to the residual analysis indicating near-i.i.d. behavior after conditioning on known drivers.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Historical backtests reveal meaningful model-order reversals or error inflation under rolling-origin evaluation, or residual dependence remains substantial at the 15-minute horizon after conditioning.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Historical analyses show low residual dependence at the target horizon and near-equivalent model ranking and error between blocked-random and rolling-origin evaluation for the same retraining cadence.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Average error differences are small but extreme-weather windows behave differently, leaving uncertainty about whether blocked-random evaluation fully captures tail conditions.

must_not_claim: Any time-series benchmark that does not use a purely chronological split is automatically invalid.
The team must be leaking future information because evaluation windows were randomized.
Failure to test long-horizon seasonal drift invalidates a 15-minute-ahead operational forecasting claim.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Blocked random evaluation windows in a forecasting study; explicit reliance on short-horizon residual near-independence; evaluation target limited to 15-minute-ahead forecasts with nightly retraining rather than long-range forecasting.
ground_truth_rationale: Chronological splits are a sensible default, but the prompt provides empirical evidence that for this short-horizon conditioned process they behave similarly to blocked-random evaluation. Because the claim is tightly scoped to that regime, the non-standard split is justified rather than flawed.
notes: Medium empirical-pre-analysis case for time series, designed to tempt over-application of the chronological-split heuristic.

task_prompt: A clinical ML team developed a sepsis early-warning model at one tertiary hospital and then validated it on data from one partner hospital that uses the same electronic health record vendor and nearly identical bedside documentation templates. Critics often object to single-site external validation, and this study seems vulnerable because the partner cohort is only one institution and some minority subgroups are too small for precise calibration curves. The report is narrower than a casual reading suggests. Before validation, the authors defined success as maintaining acceptable discrimination and calibration after a simple intercept recalibration using the first month of unlabeled prevalence information from the partner hospital. They did not claim universal transport across all hospital types. Instead, the stated question was whether the model could be ported to another site with the same EHR configuration, similar lab panel availability, and comparable nursing documentation cadence. On the held-out partner-hospital cohort, the recalibrated model meets the pre-specified discrimination and calibration targets, and the report explicitly states that subgroup estimates with wide intervals should be treated as descriptive only. It also states that a community hospital with different triage workflows would require a separate validation. A critic could still say that because only one external hospital is included and subgroup power is limited, the entire validation is methodologically unsound. Exonerating the study requires separating two ideas: limited transportability beyond similar hospitals is a real limitation that warrants disclosure, but it does not invalidate the narrower claim that the model was successfully validated for a closely matched site under a prospectively described recalibration procedure.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the validation claim is explicitly restricted to a closely matched partner hospital and the pre-specified recalibration-plus-evaluation procedure succeeded there, even though broader transportability remains limited.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Confirm that the validation protocol prospectively restricted the target setting to hospitals with the same EHR configuration and pre-specified the intercept recalibration step, then verify discrimination and calibration targets on the held-out partner-hospital cohort.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say one external hospital and weak subgroup precision make the study invalid, but that concern is invalid because the claim is explicitly limited to a closely matched site and the broad-transportability limitation is disclosed rather than ignored.

ideal_critique: Check whether the claim is truly limited to hospitals with matching EHR and workflow characteristics and whether the pre-specified recalibration was applied without outcome peeking beyond the allowed prevalence information.
A calibrated critique should distinguish between insufficient evidence for broad transportability and insufficient evidence for the narrower same-configuration validation claim.
ideal_defense: Point to the prospectively limited deployment claim, the matched EHR configuration, and the successful held-out evaluation after the pre-specified recalibration procedure.
Concede that more diverse hospitals would need fresh validation, but argue that this disclosure does not undermine the narrower validated-use claim.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The report generalizes beyond closely matched hospitals, or the recalibrated model fails the pre-specified discrimination or calibration targets on the held-out partner site.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The protocol and writeup keep the claim restricted to a closely matched partner hospital, and the held-out partner-site evaluation meets the pre-specified calibration and discrimination targets after the allowed recalibration step.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Overall performance meets targets but one clinically important subgroup shows unstable calibration with very wide intervals, leaving uncertainty about whether the written limitations are sufficiently prominent.

must_not_claim: Any external validation done at only one additional hospital is automatically invalid.
Limited subgroup sample size invalidates the main site-level validation claim.
A model that requires local recalibration cannot be considered externally validated even when recalibration was prospectively allowed and evaluated.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Only one external hospital; local intercept recalibration before final evaluation; subgroup estimates with wide confidence intervals; claim intentionally limited to hospitals with matching EHR and workflow characteristics.
ground_truth_rationale: This case is hard because it invites confusion between limited scope and invalid evidence. The study does not support universal hospital deployment, but it can still validly support deployment to a closely matched partner hospital if that narrow claim and recalibration procedure were specified prospectively and the held-out evaluation meets target performance.
notes: Hard medical-ML case focused on limitation-versus-invalidation reasoning. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt: A software company built an NLP routing model for customer-support emails and evaluated it only on English messages from its U.S. enterprise customers. That choice can look problematic because the company also serves some multilingual accounts, and the architecture is capable of processing multiple languages. The report, however, is careful about what is and is not being claimed. Product operations had already decided that the first launch would cover only English-language inboxes, with all other languages routed directly to human specialists. The study therefore restricted the dataset, annotation guidelines, and success criteria to English messages. Before training, the team pre-specified that deployment would remain English-only until a separate multilingual annotation effort and language-specific calibration study were completed. The final test set consists of a future month of U.S. enterprise emails, and the model is only claimed to improve routing accuracy and response-time triage in that English workflow. The report openly states that it provides no evidence about other languages, smaller self-serve customers, or non-U.S. legal terminology. It also includes a fairness appendix using available role and account-size labels within the English population, while noting that some demographic dimensions are unobserved. A critic might still argue that because the vendor's overall product is global and demographic coverage is incomplete, the benchmark is invalid or biased on its face. Exonerating the work requires noticing that absence of evidence for unclaimed populations is not the same as evidence against the validity of the explicitly English-only deployment claim, and that missing labels for some fairness dimensions create a disclosure obligation rather than automatically nullifying the in-scope test result.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the evaluation is explicitly scoped to an English-only deployment population, and the lack of evidence for other languages or unobserved fairness dimensions is a limitation that is disclosed rather than a flaw that invalidates the in-scope claim.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Verify that product documentation and the pre-specified launch plan restricted deployment to English messages, then confirm that the future-month English test set reflects that population and that the report does not generalize the result to other languages.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say the lack of multilingual or fully observed fairness evaluation makes the benchmark invalid, but that concern is invalid because the claim is explicitly limited to an English-only deployment population and the remaining gaps are disclosed as out-of-scope limitations.

ideal_critique: Check whether the writeup truly keeps the claim English-only and whether unsupported groups or fairness dimensions are presented as limitations rather than silently ignored.

A calibrated critique should push for clearer disclosure or future work on multilingual and missing-label fairness issues without treating those omissions as automatic refutation of the in-scope result.
ideal_defense: Explain that the deployed workflow excludes non-English messages by design, so English-only evaluation is aligned with the launch population.
Concede that multilingual and some fairness questions remain unanswered, but emphasize that these are scope boundaries already stated in the report.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The product actually deploys the model to non-English messages or the report makes cross-language claims unsupported by the English-only test set.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Launch documentation and the report consistently restrict the model to English-only deployment, and the future-month test set represents that English population without unsupported generalization.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The main results are English-only but marketing language elsewhere loosely implies broader applicability, creating uncertainty about the true claim boundary.

must_not_claim: An English-only evaluation is automatically invalid because the company serves some multilingual customers.
Missing labels for some fairness dimensions necessarily invalidate all routing performance claims.
A model architecture capable of multiple languages must be validated in every supported language before any narrower launch claim can be tested.

suspicious_but_valid_features: English-only dataset despite a global product; fairness appendix limited to observed labels; explicit non-deployment of other languages to the model; claims narrowed to U.S. enterprise English email routing.
ground_truth_rationale: The tricky part is recognizing that missing evidence outside the stated scope does not automatically undermine an in-scope result. The benchmark is sound because the deployment population is explicitly English-only, the future-month test reflects that population, and out-of-scope fairness and multilingual gaps are disclosed rather than hidden.
notes: Hard NLP case centered on scope discipline and limitation-versus-invalidation reasoning. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt: A lender is testing a tabular risk model that predicts whether a consumer loan application will become 90-days delinquent. The feature list includes several missingness indicators, such as whether income documentation was absent at submission and whether the external bureau returned a thin-file code. Those variables can look suspicious because critics often associate missingness with target leakage or with hidden use of downstream manual-review outcomes. The study's methods section draws a sharper line. All missingness indicators are computed at the moment the application packet is submitted, before any underwriting decision or collection outcome is observed. The model does not use manual-review notes, final approval decisions, or any post-decision interventions. The reason for keeping the indicators is operational: incomplete documentation and bureau thin files are genuinely available at scoring time and affect near-term default risk in this underwriting workflow. The benchmark evaluates the frozen model on a future quarter of applications using exactly the information available at submission time. The report also includes a fairness audit over the bank's monitored protected-class proxies and states clearly that a different institution with different document-collection practices would need separate validation. A critic might still argue that because the model uses missingness patterns, it is necessarily leaking downstream process information or is methodologically invalid as a credit model. Exonerating the work requires distinguishing between a legitimate predictive feature available at decision time and an impermissible post-outcome proxy. The study may still deserve disclosure about workflow dependence and fairness monitoring, but those concerns do not by themselves invalidate the in-scope predictive evaluation described here.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the missingness indicators are legitimate application-time features available before any decision or outcome, and the evaluation is limited to the same underwriting workflow in which those signals exist.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Audit feature-generation timestamps to confirm that all missingness indicators are created at application submission and exclude any post-decision or post-outcome information, then verify future-quarter test performance using the frozen submission-time feature set.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say that missingness indicators necessarily encode leakage or invalid downstream process signals, but that concern is invalid here because the indicators are generated at application time and exclude any post-decision information.

ideal_critique: Check feature-generation timestamps carefully to ensure that no manual-review outcomes, approval decisions, or later interventions contaminate the submission-time missingness indicators.
A calibrated critique may note workflow dependence and fairness considerations, but should distinguish those from a claim of target leakage if the indicators are genuinely available at scoring time.
ideal_defense: Explain that missingness itself can be a legitimate application-time risk signal when it is observed before the lending decision and frozen consistently across train and test.
Point to the timestamp audit, the future-quarter holdout, and the explicit statement that the result only supports the same underwriting workflow.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Feature lineage shows that any missingness indicator depends on manual-review outcomes, approval decisions, or other information unavailable at application submission.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Timestamped lineage confirms that all missingness indicators are derived solely from submission-time application fields and bureau response codes, and the frozen model is evaluated on a future-quarter holdout from the same workflow.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Most indicators are clearly pre-decision but one feature has incomplete lineage documentation, leaving uncertainty about whether it is purely submission-time.

must_not_claim: Any missingness indicator in a tabular risk model is automatically target leakage.
Using submission-time documentation gaps makes the benchmark invalid because the feature may reflect institution workflow.
A predictive credit-risk model is methodologically unsound unless every feature is causally interpretable rather than merely available at decision time.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Missingness indicators in a credit model; bureau thin-file codes that resemble process artifacts; strong emphasis on feature timestamp lineage; claim explicitly restricted to the same underwriting workflow and document-collection process.
ground_truth_rationale: The hard part is separating legitimate workflow-dependent predictive signals from true leakage. If the indicators are created strictly at application time and no post-decision information is used, then workflow dependence is a limitation to disclose, not a reason to call the evaluation invalid.
notes: Hard tabular-data case focused on distinguishing application-time process signals from genuine leakage. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt:
A public health network is considering deploying a diabetic retinopathy model in mobile screening vans that visit rural communities. Nurses would capture retinal photos, and patients above the model threshold would be referred for in-person ophthalmology follow-up within two weeks. The health network currently relies on a rule that sends many unreadable or borderline images for referral, creating long waits at the regional eye clinic. The modeling team reports strong retrospective sensitivity and specificity on 60,000 prior images collected at the network's hospital eye centers and says the model could reduce unnecessary referrals by one-third.

In the historical dataset, ophthalmologists assigned the final disease labels after reviewing images from patients who completed specialty exams. Many of those patients were originally sent for follow-up because a clinician or an earlier screening rule considered them suspicious. The mobile program would use lower-cost cameras and staff with less ophthalmic training, but the vendor says the imaging protocol is similar. The deployment plan keeps a quality-control rule: any image judged unreadable by the capture software still goes to manual review, which some board members think may hide a problem. The team also notes that disease prevalence in the rural program is expected to be lower than at the referral centers where the model was studied.

Program leaders ask whether the retrospective hospital-center results justify using the model to determine who gets scarce follow-up appointments in the mobile setting. Consider what the labels and workflow imply for prospective deployment.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The referral-center backtest is not enough to justify mobile-clinic deployment because the labels and image acquisition process likely differ from the proposed screening setting.

planted_issues.description: Final labels come primarily from patients who completed specialty exams after being flagged, so the dataset may not represent the full screening population.
The mobile program uses different cameras and less specialized staff, creating a prospective distribution shift not tested in the hospital-center backtest.
The real decision is how many scarce follow-up appointments to allocate, but the evidence emphasizes aggregate test metrics rather than threshold-specific referral burden and misses.

ideal_critique: Spot the verification-bias problem: specialty-confirmed labels depend on prior suspicion and follow-up completion.
Connect hardware and operator differences to prospective failure risk in the mobile deployment.
Frame the decision as allocating scarce referral slots, where false negatives and false positives have different consequences.
ideal_defense: Concede that unreadable-image routing to manual review is a sensible safeguard, not itself a flaw.
Argue that the model could still be useful if validated on real mobile images before referral decisions are automated.
Emphasize that lower prevalence changes thresholding needs rather than automatically invalidating the model.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a prospective mobile-van study with masked specialist adjudication on all sampled patients, sensitivity at the planned referral threshold falls below the prespecified safety floor or unreadable/manual-review rates differ substantially from the hospital dataset.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a prospective mobile-van study with independent adjudication, the model maintains the prespecified sensitivity floor while reducing referrals by at least 20% relative to the current rule and keeping unreadable-image escalation manageable.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model performs well on readable mobile images but too many cases are diverted to manual review to know the net effect on follow-up capacity.

must_not_claim: Lower disease prevalence alone means the model cannot be used.
Routing unreadable images to manual review necessarily invalidates the evaluation.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a screening deployment with scarce downstream specialist capacity, so thresholded referral consequences matter. The key weaknesses are verification bias and setting shift, while lower prevalence and unreadable-image manual review are valid considerations rather than automatic disqualifiers.
notes: Medium case. Healthcare screening domain. Red herring features: lower prevalence and unreadable-image QC rule may look suspicious but are valid. Added empirical_test_agreed as secondary resolution.

task_prompt:
A city emergency communications agency is evaluating an ML model that predicts which incoming non-English 911 calls are likely to require a live interpreter within the first minute. The current process connects every such call to an interpreter service by default, which adds cost and occasionally increases answer time. The proposed deployment would instead let dispatchers delay interpreter connection for low-risk calls while continuing immediately for higher-risk ones. The vendor presents retrospective results from archived call audio and transcripts, claiming the model could cut interpreter usage by 35% with minimal impact on high-acuity incidents.

Historical labels come from whether an interpreter was actually joined to the call and whether supervisors later marked the call as having a communication problem. Over the study period, dispatcher practices changed across shifts: some teams almost always brought interpreters in early, while others relied on bilingual staff or caller family members. The evaluation reports aggregate precision and recall but does not separate chest pain, domestic violence, welfare checks, and low-priority property incidents. The agency plans to keep a rule that any caller who sounds distressed, disconnected repeatedly, or cannot confirm location still gets an immediate interpreter, regardless of model score. Some reviewers have focused on whether background noise and accent variation will reduce transcription quality, while the vendor argues the audio model does not rely entirely on ASR output.

Agency leadership asks whether the retrospective evidence justifies using the model to delay interpreter connection in production. Consider the deployment framing, not just whether historical labels are predictable.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective study does not justify delaying interpreter connection because the labels are policy-driven and the evaluation ignores the asymmetric risk of misses in high-acuity calls.

planted_issues.description: Using whether an interpreter was joined as the main label captures prior dispatcher practice rather than the underlying communication need the model would govern prospectively.
Delaying interpretation in a critical medical or violence call is far more costly than doing so in a low-priority incident, but the evaluation reports only aggregate metrics.

ideal_critique: Recognize that the label largely reflects what dispatchers historically did, not ground truth about who truly needed interpretation immediately.
Ask for stratified evidence by call type or acuity because misses are much costlier in certain emergencies.
Note that a safe override rule helps, but does not remove the need to test the delayed-connection policy directly.
ideal_defense: Defend the override rule as a real safeguard, not evidence of leakage.
Concede that the model is better suited for decision support or workload planning until a prospective policy evaluation is done.
Argue that ASR concerns are secondary if the model demonstrably uses audio cues beyond transcripts.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a prospective pilot with mandatory case review, delayed-connection calls show any increase above the prespecified tolerance in communication failures for high-acuity incidents compared with immediate-connection controls.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a randomized dispatcher pilot, delayed-connection for low-risk calls reduces interpreter minutes by at least 20% without exceeding the prespecified non-inferiority margin on reviewed communication failures in high-acuity incidents.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Interpreter minutes fall, but reviewed communication failures are too rare or inconsistently adjudicated to determine safety.

must_not_claim: Background noise or accent variation alone proves the model cannot work.
Using an override rule means the evaluation is automatically invalid.

ground_truth_rationale: The proposed decision is to delay a service, not merely to predict historical interpreter joins. Because the labels are driven by prior dispatcher behavior and harms differ sharply by call type, aggregate retrospective metrics are not enough for deployment.
notes: Medium case. Public safety / language access domain. Red herring: override rules and concern about ASR may distract from the core framing issue.

task_prompt:
A national health insurer is deciding whether to deploy a model that automatically approves common oncology prior-authorization requests while sending others to manual review or denial. The business case is that oncologists are frustrated by delays, nurses spend large amounts of time on status calls, and medical-review staff are stretched thin. The model was trained on several years of historical authorization requests, diagnosis and regimen codes, prior approvals, and subsequent claims. The vendor's retrospective validation highlights high agreement with past approvals and says the system could auto-approve a majority of standard requests.

Under the proposed workflow, requests the model deems low-risk would be approved immediately, while the rest would either be escalated to clinical review or, for some out-of-policy regimens, denied more quickly than today. Historical labels are based on final authorization outcomes after medical-review staff applied payer policy in effect at the time. Over the study period, however, oncology policies changed repeatedly as guidelines evolved, new biosimilars entered use, and some therapies moved from inpatient to outpatient billing pathways. Urgent cases were also sometimes handled outside the standard queue with expedited physician-to-physician review, leaving less structured documentation. The validation deck reports high historical agreement and short simulated turnaround times, but it does not show performance by cancer type, line of therapy, or policy vintage.

The utilization-management committee asks whether this retrospective evidence justifies automated approvals and faster denials in production, or whether the framing of the evidence misses something important about how oncology authorization actually works.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective agreement study does not justify this deployment because historical authorization outcomes reflect shifting payer policy and incomplete urgent-case pathways rather than a stable prospective decision target.

planted_issues.description: Past authorization outcomes encode payer policy at the time, which is not the same as a stable target for future approve or deny decisions as oncology standards evolve.
Expedited physician-review pathways leave important urgent oncology cases underrepresented or poorly labeled in the historical data.
Performance is not shown by cancer type, therapy line, or policy vintage, masking where automated approvals or faster denials may be unsafe.
In oncology authorization, an incorrect fast denial can delay time-sensitive treatment in ways not reflected by historical agreement metrics.

ideal_critique: Emphasize that agreement with historical payer policy is not enough when guidelines and coverage rules move over time.
Notice that urgent cases handled outside the ordinary queue weaken the dataset precisely where denial mistakes may matter most.
Ask for analysis by cancer type, therapy line, and policy vintage before considering automation.
ideal_defense: Concede that automatic denials are much harder to justify than automatic approvals in this setting.
Defend the operational goal of reducing turnaround for clearly standard requests, while accepting that historical agreement is insufficient evidence by itself.
Suggest narrowing the use case to a small set of stable, guideline-concordant approvals with physician audit.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A prospective policy-vintage holdout or pilot shows materially worse agreement or higher physician-overturn rates in recent oncology regimens, urgent pathways, or specific cancer subgroups than the aggregate retrospective estimate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a tightly limited pilot covering only pre-specified stable regimens, auto-approvals maintain physician-overturn and appeal rates below preset limits while no automatic denials are used and subgroup performance remains within tolerance.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Aggregate pilot agreement is high but subgroup counts for urgent or newer-regimen cases are too sparse to know whether automation is safe where errors matter most.

must_not_claim: Using claims and authorization data for utilization management is automatically illegal under HIPAA.
Any disagreement with past payer policy proves the model is unusable.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a hard critique case because the deployment mixes auto-approval and faster denial in a clinically sensitive, policy-changing domain. The strongest objections come from shifting decision targets, missing urgent pathways, and asymmetric treatment-delay harms, not from generic objections to using authorization data.
notes: Hard healthcare utilization-management case requiring clinical and policy context. Multiple interacting high-severity issues and domain-plausible must-not-claim. Relabeled from hard to medium: single-pass assessor fails only 1 rubric dimension.

task_prompt:
Clinical Informatics Quarterly Model Review — ICU Sepsis Escalation Program. Across the 2019-2024 ICU warehouse, the temporal transformer is emerging as the strongest candidate to replace the legacy LSTM used in our deterioration alerting stack. On the internal benchmark, the transformer improved AUROC by 0.034 and alert-rate-matched early-warning recall by 6.1 points, and the direction held on the Hospital B validation cohort. Hospital B contributed 1,126 ICU episodes, which biostatistics confirmed was adequately powered for AUROC estimation; 95% CI width was 0.047 externally and 0.018 internally. All model development and review were completed inside the HIPAA-compliant analytics enclave with de-identified study outputs only.

Infrastructure modernization was completed in parallel with the model refresh. As part of the new episode service, ICU transfers separated by under four hours are materialized as one longitudinal care episode so monitoring is continuous across bed moves and vendor-specific encounter IDs. The shared study table was then partitioned by random encounter-level assignment across all retained episodes, consistent with our standard benchmarking service for repeated ICU utilization over multi-year histories.

Data quality harmonization also improved label fidelity. In the modernized registry, positive cases are defined as septic shock onset within 12 hours using the harmonized shock endpoint agreed with critical care operations. For reference, the incumbent LSTM had been introduced under the prior severe-sepsis program and was originally documented against a 24-hour severe-sepsis prediction target, which is why its historical summary statistics remain in the comparison appendix. A potential concern raised in review was lactate and vasopressor availability, but those fields are only admitted when observed before the prediction cutoff, so the temporal guardrail is intact.

Pipeline specification changes were otherwise limited to timestamp provenance. Where both values are available from the lab interface, serum creatinine is indexed to specimen collection time rather than later chart filing time, with both timestamps preserved for audit. Given the internal lift, the aligned external result, and cleaner operational plumbing, leadership is asking whether the evidence now supports promoting the transformer as the platform standard.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There are serious benchmark flaws, but the new model could still be better after a controlled rerun on a harmonized cohort and patient-level split.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Rebuild a single cohort with one shared endpoint definition, one timestamping policy, and one patient-level chronological split, then rerun both models and report AUROC and early-warning recall on the same internal and external cohorts.

planted_issues.description: The challenger is benchmarked under a 12-hour septic-shock endpoint while the baseline originates from a 24-hour severe-sepsis target, so the prediction task changed.
Backfilling creatinine to specimen time only in the refreshed pipeline changes feature timing and can alter apparent lead time relative to the baseline.
A random encounter-level split allows repeat ICU patients to appear in both train and test, inflating performance for both models and especially harming comparability to the older study design.
Episode merging and cohort refresh were introduced simultaneously with the model change, so the benchmark cannot isolate architecture gains from cohort-definition changes.

ideal_critique: Recognize that the target endpoint, episode construction, and timestamp policy all changed at once, so the reported gain is not a clean model comparison.
Notice that repeated ICU patients create a patient-level leakage risk when splitting at the encounter level.
Argue for a harmonized rerun rather than claiming the transformer is definitely worse.
ideal_defense: Concede the cohort and label harmonization problems and the need for patient-level splitting.
Correctly push back on the claim that lactate or vasopressor features are automatically target leakage if they are restricted to the pre-prediction window.
Emphasize that the external cohort and clinically plausible features mean the new model remains promising pending a fair rerun.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After rebuilding a shared cohort with patient-level chronological splitting, the transformer's AUROC advantage drops below 0.01 and early-warning recall at matched alert rate no longer exceeds the baseline on either hospital.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On the harmonized cohort with patient-level chronological splitting and identical endpoint definitions, the transformer still leads by at least 0.02 AUROC and at least 5 recall points at the same alert rate on both the internal and external cohorts.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The harmonized rerun keeps a modest internal gain but not the external gain, or preserves AUROC while erasing the alert-rate-matched recall gain.

must_not_claim: Including lactate or vasopressor variables is automatically target leakage because they reflect clinician concern.
The external Hospital B result is unusable simply because the validation cohort has only about 1,100 episodes.
HIPAA-compliant handling or confidence intervals resolve the fairness problems in the benchmark by themselves.

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case requires noticing multiple interacting clinical evaluation changes, not just a generic complaint about repeated patients. The right stance is mixed: the current benchmark is unreliable, but it is still plausible that the transformer remains better once endpoint definitions, timestamps, and split policies are harmonized.
notes: Hard mixed case framed as a polished quarterly review memo. The main flaws are dispersed across infrastructure, data quality, and appendix-style comparison details, while the external validation set, confidence intervals, HIPAA controls, and time-windowed lactate/vasopressor features create plausible defense evidence.
revision_notes: Recast as a quarterly clinical informatics success memo with soundness cues, separate infrastructure/pipeline sections, and the endpoint mismatch split across different paragraphs. Patient overlap is now only inferable from the encounter-level split description, and the lactate/vasopressor concern is positioned as a tempting but ultimately invalid leakage claim.

task_prompt:
Retention Program Experiment Readout — Q2 Renewal Offer Policy. The headline result from the completed discount experiment is encouraging: under offline replay, the proposed causal targeting policy is estimated to improve 28-day contribution margin by 4.8% versus the incumbent contact rule, with modeled retention up 1.9 points and a projected quarterly margin benefit of $2.3M if deployed at current scale. Finance and CRM operations both support moving forward because the result holds across major geographies and the confidence interval on estimated margin lift remains above break-even in the consolidated view.

Methodologically, the team used a doubly robust off-policy framework so that both outcome modeling and randomized treatment assignment are incorporated in the estimate. Response, cost, and propensity nuisance models were fit once on the full experiment log to maximize stability, including the final reporting period that supplied the headline tables, and evaluation was then stratified by region and week before aggregation. Treatment probabilities varied across regions because local campaign budgets were capped independently, but assignment remained randomized within each region-week cell, which is expected for this multi-region operating model rather than a protocol violation.

The business case has also matured since the original scorecard for the legacy model. Historically, the incumbent logistic model was managed against 14-day renewal retention because leadership primarily wanted to prevent churn. As discount costs and service contacts became more material, the current readout shifted to margin-weighted retention over 28 days, net of offer cost and downstream support burden, to reflect the economics the business now cares about.

For deployment configuration, operations requested a single global contact threshold that fits current staffing. The selected cutoff is therefore the one that maximized estimated incremental profit on the reporting slice while keeping weekly send volume inside the approved operational band. The legacy policy is shown at its existing production cutoff for context. On that basis, the readout recommends promotion of the causal policy for the next renewal cycle, pending final review of whether the benchmark is already persuasive enough for leadership sign-off.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The current offline benchmark is compromised by changed objectives and holdout reuse, but the causal policy could still be superior if re-evaluated under a clean protocol.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Using the randomized experiment log, refit nuisance models and policy cutoffs without the final evaluation slice, evaluate both policies on the same frozen 28-day profit metric within the randomized holdout, and report profit lift with block-aware uncertainty by region and week.

planted_issues.description: The baseline policy was historically tuned for 14-day retention while the challenger is benchmarked on 28-day net contribution margin, so the optimization target changed with the model.
Doubly robust nuisance models and propensities were fit using the full experiment log including the final reporting slice, leaking information into the offline evaluation.
The contact threshold was selected to maximize profit on the same slice used for headline evaluation, contaminating the benchmark.
The baseline policy was not re-optimized for the new 28-day margin objective before comparison, so the evaluation protocol favors the challenger.

ideal_critique: Identify that the benchmark changes both the business objective and the evaluation protocol at the same time.
Recognize that offline policy evaluation requires strict holdout discipline for nuisance fitting and cutoff selection.
Ask for a clean randomized-holdout policy comparison on one predeclared profit metric before accepting the conclusion.
ideal_defense: Concede the holdout reuse and objective-mismatch issues.
Push back on claims that region-specific treatment rates automatically invalidate doubly robust evaluation when randomization occurred within region and week.
Argue that the causal policy remains plausible and should be retested under a common profit objective rather than dismissed outright.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: On a frozen 28-day profit metric with nuisance fitting and cutoff selection excluding the final evaluation slice, the causal policy's estimated profit lift over the re-optimized baseline falls below zero or its 95% interval includes zero.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Under a clean holdout protocol on the same 28-day profit metric, the causal policy beats the re-optimized baseline by at least 3% incremental profit and the block-aware 95% interval excludes zero.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The clean rerun shows a positive but under-3% profit lift or heterogeneous gains that are positive in some regions and negative in others.

must_not_claim: Doubly robust evaluation is invalid whenever treatment probabilities differ across regions.
Any use of net contribution margin instead of retention is automatically the wrong business metric.
The presence of randomized assignment alone guarantees that off-policy estimates cannot leak information from the reporting window.

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case turns on experimentation-specific evaluation hygiene: the objective changed, the reporting slice was reused for fitting and cutoff choice, and the baseline was not re-optimized under the new metric. Because the data come from a randomized experiment, a clean re-analysis could still validate the causal policy, so the calibrated answer is mixed rather than outright rejection.
notes: Hard mixed case framed as a polished retention readout. The memo sounds rigorous and commercially grounded, but the real benchmark flaws are distributed across methods, business-case evolution, and deployment configuration. Regional treatment-rate variation is included as a strong red herring because it is expected under region-week randomization.
revision_notes: Rewrote the case as a business-forward experiment readout led by retention and margin impact. The objective shift is buried inside an 'evolving business case' paragraph, nuisance fitting on the full log is described as methodological thoroughness, and cutoff tuning appears in a deployment section so the reader must connect multiple sections to see the leakage and asymmetry issues.

task_prompt:
Fraud Ops Migration Readiness Assessment — Graph Detector Release. The migration review for the new graph-based card fraud detector is materially positive. Relative to the production gradient-boosted tree, the challenger improved precision by 3.4 points at the target weekly investigation volume and lifted PR-AUC by 0.007 on the most recent quarter. Fraud analytics recommends PR-AUC as the primary discrimination metric because of the network's extreme class imbalance and notes that this follows the ISO 8583 evaluation guidance used in our internal fraud governance playbook. Merchant alias consolidation also performed as intended during validation, reducing duplicate entities and improving downstream graph neighborhood quality rather than removing meaningful merchant variation.

Data quality work was completed in the same release train. To improve label maturation, the reporting set for the challenger excludes authorizations whose related disputes were still unresolved at 60 days, so confirmed labels reflect settled fraud outcomes instead of partially matured cases. This change tightened fraud-review agreement in manual audit.

Infrastructure was upgraded as well. The new scoring service consumes the real-time authorizations stream and applies deduplication on PAN, merchant, amount, and timestamp after merchant alias normalization, which aligns the graph model to the feed expected in production. Historical baseline figures in the migration packet continue to come from the current dashboard on the raw transaction feed, since that is the benchmark operations has been using to manage the live system.

For deployment, investigators asked that the new model land at exactly 18,000 alerts per week so staffing remains stable through the holiday quarter. The operating threshold shown in the packet was therefore calibrated on Q4 to hit that alert budget while maximizing observed precision at volume. The baseline threshold was not re-opened because the production queue is already tuned and approved. Taken together, the readiness memo presents the graph detector as a clear upgrade and asks whether risk leadership can treat the packet as sufficient evidence for migration approval.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The benchmark does not fairly compare models because feed definition, label maturity, and threshold selection all changed with the challenger.

planted_issues.description: Excluding unresolved disputes for the challenger but not the baseline changes label maturity and can bias apparent fraud performance.
The challenger is scored on a deduplicated authorizations-only feed while the baseline number comes from a raw dashboard feed, so the evaluation population changed.
The graph model's alert threshold was selected on the same Q4 quarter used for final reporting, contaminating precision-at-volume results.
Graph features and merchant-normalization preprocessing were introduced simultaneously without rerunning the baseline under the new feed rules, preventing attribution of gains.

ideal_critique: Notice that fraud label maturity and deduplication rules are changing at the same time as the model.
Recognize that precision at a fixed alert budget is especially vulnerable when the threshold is tuned on the reporting quarter itself.
Recommend rescoring both models on the same deduplicated feed with the same maturity window and a threshold chosen on prior data.
ideal_defense: Concede that class imbalance is not the central flaw and that the new pipeline may still be operationally useful.
Argue only for a common-feed rerun rather than accepting the current dashboard-to-project comparison as proof.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After both models are rescored on the same deduplicated authorizations feed with the same 60-day maturity rule and thresholds fixed from earlier quarters, the precision-at-volume gain falls below 1 point or reverses.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On the same deduplicated feed with the same maturity horizon and predeclared thresholds, the graph model improves precision at 18,000 alerts per week by at least 3 points and improves PR-AUC by at least 0.005.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A fair rerun preserves only one of the two gains, such as precision-at-volume improving while PR-AUC changes by less than 0.005.

must_not_claim: PR-AUC is unusable for fraud because the positive class is too rare.
Merchant alias merging is automatically invalid because it removes true behavioral variation.
Using an authorizations stream is itself evidence of unfairness, regardless of whether both models are evaluated on the same feed.

ground_truth_rationale: This hard case requires operational fraud knowledge about dispute maturation and transaction-feed definitions. The new system may be stronger, but the deck does not establish that because threshold choice, label maturity, and feed construction all changed with the challenger.
notes: Hard critique case framed as a polished fraud-ops migration memo. The benchmark sounds operationally mature, but the decisive flaws are hidden across data quality, feed modernization, and deployment calibration sections. PR-AUC and merchant alias consolidation are deliberately tempting but valid details.
revision_notes: Recast as a migration-readiness assessment that confidently recommends the graph model. The label-maturation rule, feed definition change, and threshold tuning are separated into data quality, infrastructure, and deployment sections so the flaws are no longer announced as explicit 'changes.' Plausible red herrings were added around PR-AUC and merchant alias resolution.

task_prompt:
Perception Milestone Review — ADAS Pedestrian Program. The latest pedestrian detector cleared the planned perception milestone and is being positioned as the strongest vision gain in this release cycle. On the held-out fleet benchmark, mAP@0.5 improved from 61 to 69 overall, with the nighttime slice up 19% relative to the incumbent. Because the evaluation was run on the same forward-camera hardware, optics stack, and annotation protocol as the prior release, the team is presenting the result as a clean signal that perception quality has moved meaningfully in the scenarios most relevant to low-light pedestrian risk. Program management would like to reference the nighttime gain as evidence of a safety advancement in emergency braking situations.

The test corpus covers urban arterials, neighborhood streets, and suburban connectors, and the standard perception protocol remained unchanged: per-frame evaluation against 2D pedestrian boxes with the usual IoU matching rules, stratified by lighting condition and pedestrian size. Reviewers noted that the improvement was not concentrated in one corner case; the gain appeared across small, medium, and large boxes, with the largest relative improvement after dusk. That consistency is being cited as support for the safety narrative.

From a hardware-efficiency standpoint, the model still fits on the current compute stack, though average inference latency increased by roughly 80 ms after backbone expansion and additional temporal smoothing. Systems engineering characterized the change as manageable because memory stayed within budget and throughput remained inside the perception ECU envelope without changing camera hardware or sensor sync.

The release packet does not include planner-in-the-loop stopping-distance studies or closed-track braking results. The downstream braking stack, however, only commits when pedestrian detections arrive early enough and remain stable across successive frames, especially in low-light approach scenarios. The milestone memo therefore asks whether the benchmark evidence already justifies the stronger statement that the program is safer at night, or whether the result should be framed more narrowly as improved detector quality pending system-level validation.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The mAP metric alone is insufficient for the safety claim, but whether the detection improvement translates to better braking outcomes is an empirical question requiring planner-in-the-loop testing.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct planner-in-the-loop tests measuring nighttime braking success rate and stopping margin for the new detector versus baseline.

planted_issues.description: Overall mAP on mostly daytime frames does not directly evaluate the memo's nighttime safety claim.
Frame-level box overlap does not capture whether pedestrians are detected early and stably enough for emergency braking.
Added inference latency can offset a detection-quality gain for safety-critical braking, but mAP does not account for that interaction.

ideal_critique: Separate the narrow object-detection result from the stronger system-level safety claim.
Emphasize nighttime slice performance, detection timing, stability across frames, and end-to-end braking consequences.
Note that latency and detector quality interact in safety-critical settings and cannot be ignored just because mAP rose.
ideal_defense: Concede that mAP is a useful component metric for detector quality, but not sufficient for the memo's safety statement.
Argue that the improvement is encouraging if it also appears on the nighttime slice.
Avoid claiming that uniform mAP gains automatically translate into fewer collisions.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: When evaluated with planner-in-the-loop testing, the new detector does not improve or worsens nighttime braking outcomes because detection timing and latency dominate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Planner-in-the-loop tests show statistically significant improvement in nighttime braking success and stopping margin despite the added latency.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves some nighttime scenarios but trades them off against others, leaving overall safety impact unclear without policy choices.

must_not_claim: using_same_camera_hardware_is_invalid
all_box_metrics_are_useless
The latency increase can be ignored automatically because compute remains within the ECU budget.

ground_truth_rationale: The memo makes an end-to-end safety claim about a specific nighttime failure mode, while the headline metric is an aggregate frame-level detection score. In this setting, slice-specific recall, detection timing, stability, and latency-sensitive braking outcomes are the relevant evidence.
notes: Hard mixed case framed as a confident perception milestone memo. Same-hardware testing and the broad nighttime mAP gain create a plausible success narrative, but the reviewer must connect the per-frame metric and added latency to the much stronger emergency-braking safety claim.
revision_notes: Reframed as an ADAS milestone review that opens with the mAP headline and treats the nighttime gain as a safety advancement. The crucial timing mismatch is buried inside the standard per-frame test protocol description, while latency is discussed in a hardware-efficiency paragraph that sounds operational rather than safety-critical.

task_prompt:
To: Hospital AI Governance Committee
From: Diagnostic Imaging Evaluation Workgroup
Subject: Vendor review summary — Chest X-ray report drafting model

Following a joint review by radiology, informatics, compliance, and patient safety, the workgroup believes the vendor's chest X-ray drafting system has generated encouraging evidence for broader controlled use. Across 380 retrospectively sampled studies from three hospitals, the system improved the primary concordance metric from 0.81 to 0.89, an 8.3-point gain with a reported 95% confidence interval of ±4.2 points. The vendor's pre-specified power analysis targeted 360 studies for 80% power, so the reviewed sample exceeded the planned threshold. Workflow proxy results were similarly strong: relative to the finalized attending reports, the generated drafts showed 87% fewer keystrokes than the current template-first process. Appendix B also reports inter-rater agreement of κ=0.71 on finding labels, which thoracic section leadership considered within the normal range for radiology annotation.

Qualitative feedback from radiologists was favorable. Dr. Meera S. wrote that for routine chest films the draft “usually gets me to an acceptable first pass faster than dictating from a blank screen.” Dr. Alan P. commented that the tool “captures the common cardiopulmonary findings reliably enough that I am mostly refining language rather than rebuilding the report.” In a blinded side-by-side review of 60 cases, 11 of 14 radiologists preferred the model-assisted first draft over the current template workflow for initial readability, and the governance review did not identify any patient-safety event requiring immediate escalation.

The executive summary therefore states that the model improves report quality and should materially lighten reporting workload for routine chest X-rays. Technical Appendix A defines the primary accuracy endpoint as finding-level concordance on labeled categories including pleural effusion, cardiomegaly, edema, consolidation, pneumothorax, support devices, and other common chest findings. The workflow appendix calculates the keystroke reduction from normalized character-level difference between the generated draft and the final attending-signed report. The committee also reviewed subgroup variance, rare-finding examples, and the vendor's quality-management controls before forwarding this recommendation.

Please assess whether the evidence, as presented, is sufficient to support the stated deployment claim, or whether a narrower conclusion or additional validation step is still warranted.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported metrics are encouraging but not sufficient to conclude clinical acceptability or reduced editing burden without a targeted radiologist workflow study.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a blinded reader study measuring radiologist editing time, clinically important omission or wording errors, and final report acceptance compared with the current workflow.

planted_issues.description: The memo treats finding-level concordance in the appendix as support for an executive-summary claim about overall report quality, even though finding labels do not cover all clinically important aspects of a radiology report.
The advertised “87% fewer keystrokes” is derived from a text-difference proxy against final reports, not from direct measurement of actual radiologist time or cognitive editing burden.
The memo sounds comprehensive but presents no controlled workflow study directly measuring time-to-edit, final report acceptance, or clinically important error rates under real use.

ideal_critique: Notice that the executive summary claims better report quality while the technical appendix only reports finding-level concordance on labeled findings.
Explain that a normalized text-difference proxy or “keystroke reduction” is not the same as measured editing time or cognitive burden for radiologists.
Ask for a blinded workflow or reader study that directly measures time-to-edit, clinically important omissions, and final report acceptance before broader deployment.
ideal_defense: Acknowledge that the committee assembled meaningful positive evidence, including multi-site sampling, power analysis, and favorable radiologist impressions.
Treat κ=0.71 label agreement and the ±4.2 point confidence interval as context, not as fatal flaws, because they can be reasonable in radiology benchmarking.
Defend a controlled prospective validation phase for routine chest X-rays rather than an unconditional claim that report quality and workload impact are already proven.
