ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a blinded workflow study, radiologists save no time or the model introduces clinically important omissions or wording errors at unacceptable rates.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A controlled workflow study shows statistically significant editing-time reduction with non-inferior clinically important error rates and high final report acceptance.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Editing time improves for routine cases but not for complex studies, or acceptance depends strongly on radiologist subgroup or case mix.

must_not_claim: confidence_intervals_are_suspicious
inter_rater_agreement_below_one_means_benchmark_is_invalid
kappa_0_71_is_unacceptably_low_for_radiology_annotation
the_380_study_sample_is_obviously_underpowered_just_because_the_f1_ci_looks_wide

ground_truth_rationale: This remains a mixed case because the memo contains real positive evidence and plausible stakeholder support. The buried problem is that the headline claim is about report quality and workload, while the core quantitative evidence is finding-level concordance plus a text-difference proxy. A prospective radiologist workflow study is still needed to settle the deployment claim.
notes: Hard mixed case requiring domain knowledge about radiology reporting and why label agreement or edit distance do not fully capture report utility. Domain expertise required: radiology (report quality standards, radiologist workflow norms).
revision_notes: Rewrote the prompt as a confident AI-governance memo with executive-summary claims, radiologist endorsements, appendix language, and procedural detail. Buried the key mismatch by separating “report quality” in the summary from finding-level concordance in a technical appendix, and reframed edit distance as “87% fewer keystrokes” so the missing time-to-edit study is only noticeable by absence.

task_prompt:
To: Procurement Steering Committee
From: Procurement Analytics and System Operations
Subject: Day-ahead load forecasting vendor recommendation

After completing a 14-month evaluation, Procurement Analytics recommends selecting GridSight as the utility's day-ahead load forecasting vendor. On normalized MAPE, GridSight delivered 4.8% versus 5.6% for the incumbent, with lower error in 11 of 14 operating months and 17 of 21 load zones. The evaluation window of March 2024 through May 2025 was approved in advance for operational completeness because it covered spring shoulder demand, summer peak operations, and a full winter planning cycle under the current EMS and bidding workflow. Following NERC reliability reporting practice, extreme outage days that triggered emergency curtailment were excluded from the MAPE calculation and reviewed separately. Both vendors used day-ahead weather forecasts as inputs, which matches actual scheduling operations.

The assessment combined archived incumbent outputs and live shadow forecasting. Incumbent NorthPeak files were reconstructed from its final 05:00 day-ahead package for the pre-transition period, while GridSight results were taken from the 06:30 package now submitted to Energy Scheduling ahead of the ISO nomination deadline. GridSight remained below 5% MAPE through the July-August peak and showed narrower error tails on high-load days. The selected March 2024 through May 2025 window also captured three of the territory's top-ten summer load days and a winter season with only two cold-weather conservation alerts, giving the team confidence that the vendor was tested across meaningful operating conditions.

Background system conditions also improved during the review period. Following grid reliability improvements completed in Q1 2024, unplanned outage frequency declined 31%, reducing dispatch disruptions and OMS data interruptions. Operations views that decline as positive evidence that the evaluation reflects the grid state the winning vendor will actually support. Finance reviewed zone-level confidence intervals, procurement reviewed contract comparability, and no zone showed statistically significant deterioration relative to the incumbent.

Based on the breadth of this review, the team believes the evidence is sufficient to support transition to GridSight, pending final commercial negotiations. Please assess whether that recommendation is justified by the evaluation as presented, or whether additional matched testing is still needed before award.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The new vendor may be better, but the current comparison is too confounded to show how much of the error reduction came from the model rather than the operating period.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a matched rolling-origin backtest or parallel forecast bakeoff on the same dates, same submission cutoff, and stratified weather/outage conditions.

planted_issues.description: The memo presents a thorough 14-month review, but it compares vendor performance across different calendar periods and weather regimes rather than on the same dates.
The post-Q1 2024 reduction in outages is framed as good news, but it also means the new vendor was evaluated under easier operating conditions than the incumbent period.
The archived incumbent forecasts came from a 05:00 package while the new vendor was evaluated on a 06:30 package, so the comparison may use different effective information cutoffs.

ideal_critique: Identify that the strongest missing element is a same-date bakeoff with both vendors using the same operating conditions and submission cutoff.
Explain why comparing different weather regimes and a post-reliability-improvement outage environment can distort MAPE differences.
Notice that the 05:00 versus 06:30 package detail matters because later day-ahead runs can incorporate better information even when both are operationally legitimate.
ideal_defense: Acknowledge that the evaluation includes real strengths such as a long review window, zone-level analysis, and operationally standard inputs.
Treat use of day-ahead weather forecasts and exclusion of extreme outage days under NERC-style reporting as normal practice rather than the central problem.
Defend running a matched rolling-origin backtest or live parallel bakeoff before final commitment, not claiming the observed MAPE gain is already cleanly causal.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a same-date backtest with matched submission cutoff and weather regimes, the error gap shrinks below 0.2 percentage points or reverses.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Across at least one year of common-date forecasts with matched cutoff rules, the new vendor beats the old by at least 0.7 percentage points overall and within major weather regimes.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The new vendor wins overall but only on easier weather days or only when allowed a later effective information cutoff.

must_not_claim: using_weather_forecasts_as_inputs_is_automatically_leakage
excluding_outage_days_is_always_the_correct_fix_regardless_of_procurement_use_case
excluding_extreme_outage_days_from_mape_under_nerc_style_reporting_is_itself_proof_of_cherry_picking
day_ahead_weather_forecast_inputs_make_the_evaluation_invalid

ground_truth_rationale: This remains mixed because the new vendor may truly be stronger and the memo includes several reasonable evaluation practices. The hidden problem is that the apparent MAPE win is intertwined with different weather periods, fewer outages, and a different forecast package cutoff. A same-date normalized bakeoff is still needed to isolate model quality.
notes: Hard mixed temporal case requiring forecasting-domain knowledge about weather regimes and submission cutoffs. Domain expertise required: energy sector (seasonal weather regime effects on load forecasting).
revision_notes: Rewrote the case as a polished procurement memo emphasizing a 14-month review, headline MAPE improvement, and operational realism. Buried the confounding by placing the weather-regime clues, outage decline, and 05:00 versus 06:30 package detail in separate paragraphs so the reviewer must connect them.

task_prompt:
To: Marketplace Leadership Team
From: Product Analytics
Subject: Smart Shortlist pilot retrospective and launch recommendation

Product Analytics recommends moving Smart Shortlist from pilot to broader commercial release based on strong early marketplace outcomes. During the February-through-May pilot window, employers that used Smart Shortlist recorded a 22% higher interview-to-application rate than employers remaining on the standard candidate review flow. The staged rollout was designed with adoption discipline in mind: employers opted into Smart Shortlist during the pilot, activation proceeded in regional waves, and reporting closed only after each cohort had at least three full weeks of usage. Weekly model retraining followed a fixed temporal cutoff, using data available through the prior Sunday at 23:59 UTC so no same-week interview outcomes entered the next recommendation build.

Adoption was strongest among employers already leaning into structured hiring workflows. Accounts using our full ATS integration suite activated fastest because setup was nearly one click, and this segment showed the largest gain in interview efficiency. Customer success notes also indicate that pilot employers were especially engaged with job setup, response-time SLAs, and recruiter seat utilization, which helped the team collect feedback quickly and refine onboarding materials. Regional enablement launched on a staggered schedule across North America, EMEA, and APAC, allowing sales and support teams to standardize messaging before each wave expanded.

The pilot launched in February and the reporting window closed in May. That timing allowed us to observe Smart Shortlist through the platform's busiest early-career and graduate hiring cycle, when employer demand and candidate responsiveness are both elevated. Across the full sample, integrated employers, mid-market accounts, and enterprise customers each showed positive directional lift, with the strongest results concentrated in accounts that reviewed recommendations at least twice per week. In user interviews, employers consistently described the feature as helping recruiters reach viable candidates sooner and spend less time paging through low-intent applicants.

Given the observed lift, clean operational rollout, and favorable employer feedback, the team believes Smart Shortlist is ready for broader launch and packaging discussion. Please assess whether the evidence supports that conclusion as stated, or whether a more causal validation step is still necessary.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The feature could be helpful, but the current evidence is too confounded by adoption patterns and recruiting-season timing to justify a strong causal claim.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a randomized encouragement or staged rollout, or a matched difference-in-differences analysis comparing adopters and non-adopters across the same recruiting periods.

planted_issues.description: The memo describes employers opting into the pilot as a rollout detail, but those early adopters are likely more engaged and higher intent than non-adopters.
The February-to-May pilot window overlaps the platform’s early-career and graduate hiring season, so outcome differences can reflect seasonal demand and role mix rather than the feature alone.
Stronger results among employers using the full ATS integration suite may partly reflect richer data and more operational maturity, not just the recommender itself.
The staggered regional launch means adoption and performance may co-move with sales and customer-success enablement timing rather than with feature impact alone.

ideal_critique: Recognize that pilot participation is a treatment-choice variable because employers opted in rather than being randomly assigned.
Connect the February-to-May timing with graduate hiring season and notice that the strongest segment is also the one with full ATS integration and richer operational data.
Argue for a randomized encouragement design, matched difference-in-differences, or similarly causal rollout analysis before turning the pilot lift into a strong launch or pricing claim.
ideal_defense: Acknowledge that the product team assembled plausible positive evidence, including meaningful business metrics, segmented reads, and proper retraining cutoffs.
Treat weekly retraining with a prior-week cutoff as standard practice rather than the core flaw.
Defend the feature as promising enough for a cleaner causal test or limited rollout, not as already proven to drive the observed 22% lift for the full employer base.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A matched diff-in-diff or randomized encouragement design shows little or no interview-rate improvement once recruiting season and integration status are balanced.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Across matched employers in the same recruiting windows, encouraged or randomly enabled accounts show a persistent interview-rate lift of at least 10% after adoption.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The feature helps some integrated enterprise employers, but the average quarter-level gap largely reflects adoption and seasonal mix.

must_not_claim: weekly_retraining_is_itself_evidence_of_leakage
interview_to_application_rate_is_automatically_an_invalid_business_metric
the_prior_sunday_temporal_cutoff_still_proves_leakage_without_further_argument
results_from_fully_integrated_employers_can_be_treated_as_unbiased_evidence_for_all_employers

ground_truth_rationale: This is still a mixed causal-inference case. The feature may be genuinely useful, and the memo includes real operational detail that makes the results believable. But the observed lift is entangled with self-selected early adopters, ATS integration status, regional rollout timing, and a February-to-May labor-market window that is unusually favorable for hiring activity.
notes: Hard mixed causal-inference case with temporal and self-selection confounds.
revision_notes: Reframed the case as a confident product retrospective with a staged rollout, segment cuts, and employer quotes. Buried the self-selection and seasonal confounding inside normal pilot language such as employers opting in, February-to-May reporting, stronger results for fully integrated accounts, and staggered regional enablement.

task_prompt:
To: Internal Audit Leadership and Audit Committee
From: Model Risk and Continuous Monitoring
Subject: FY2024 annual performance report — journal entry anomaly model

The journal entry anomaly model delivered materially stronger audit-screening results in FY2024 and is now a candidate to become the primary prioritization layer for manual review. Using the legacy rules queue as the reference point from the first half of the year, the model-enabled workflow in the second half increased the detection rate of escalated entries from 38% to 61% and improved precision from 14% to 23%. Performance was strongest in Q4, when the model surfaced a higher share of entries that internal audit ultimately advanced for deeper investigation or post-close adjustment. Coverage also improved: internal audit reviewed 100% of model-flagged entries within SLA throughout active deployment.

The evaluation was documented across the full 12-month audit cycle. Q1 and Q2 established the baseline under the legacy screen, Q3 introduced the model into the review queue, and Q4 measured performance under year-end close conditions. Appendix tables show that quarter-end volume spikes persisted as expected and that reviewer staffing was temporarily increased during close support, but leadership did not identify any operational obstacle to continued use. From an efficiency standpoint, the model concentrated analyst attention into a smaller, more productive queue while preserving review cadence.

The compliance section notes one broader control change implemented during the year. Following the Q2 controls enhancement program, all journal entries above $50K now require dual sign-off before posting. Finance considers this a separate governance improvement that should reduce downstream investigation burden regardless of analytics tooling. The report also emphasizes that the model performed especially well in Q4, including on manual accruals, reserves, and other year-end adjustments that matter most to the external-audit interface.

Based on the observed gains, Model Risk and Internal Audit recommend making the anomaly model the primary screening mechanism for journal-entry review in FY2025. Please assess whether the evidence in this annual report supports that conclusion, or whether the performance claim is not yet reliable enough for that step.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The claimed precision gain is not reliable evidence of model superiority because year-end audit conditions, policy changes, and model-influenced labeling all changed at once.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Apply the legacy screen and the model to the same journal-entry population in parallel, with stable review rules and outcome definitions across the same closing period.

planted_issues.description: The report presents Q4 outperformance as a strength, but year-end close is a materially different journal-entry environment from earlier quarters.
The dual-sign-off requirement for entries above $50K is framed as a separate control enhancement, but it changes the process and likely the label distribution during the model period.
Because internal audit reviewed 100% of model-flagged entries, the model partly determines which items receive scrutiny and therefore which positive labels are observed.
The annual report compares H1 baseline quarters with H2 model quarters but shows no same-period parallel holdout against the legacy screen.

ideal_critique: Notice that the report’s strongest claimed evidence comes from quarter-to-quarter comparisons across very different audit environments rather than from a same-period holdout.
Explain why Q4 close dynamics, temporary staffing, and the new dual-signoff rule all create competing explanations for higher precision and detection rate.
Point out that reviewing 100% of model-flagged entries is rigorous operationally but also makes the observed labels partly model-selected, which weakens the causal performance claim.
ideal_defense: Concede that the annual report is not enough to justify making the model the sole primary screen without a parallel controlled comparison.
Argue only that the model may still deserve continued use in shadow or assistive mode because the observed gains could contain some real signal.
Treat quarter-end volume spikes as standard accounting operations rather than as the central reason to reject the report.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a same-quarter parallel evaluation with stable review rules, the model's precision advantage disappears or the same gain is reproduced by process changes alone.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On the same closing-period journal population with unchanged review protocols, the model materially outperforms the legacy screen on independently adjudicated findings.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves queue efficiency somewhat, but most of the quarter-over-quarter precision increase is explained by close-period conditions and review-policy changes.

must_not_claim: quarter_end_volume_spikes_are_themselves_evidence_of_fraud
round_number_journal_entries_are_automatically_invalid_labels
quarter_end_spikes_in_journal_volume_are_abnormal_for_accounting_operations
manual_accruals_and_year_end_adjustments_are_inherently_suspicious_without_additional_context

ground_truth_rationale: This remains a critique case because several confounds all point in the same direction. The model is judged across different quarters, especially a year-end close quarter with unusual journal-entry characteristics, after a dual-approval policy change, and using outcomes from reviews that the model itself helped prioritize. Those issues are severe enough that the claimed improvement is not reliable evidence of model superiority.
notes: Hard finance/audit case with multiple interacting high-severity issues and domain-plausible must_not_claims. Domain expertise required: financial audit (year-end close seasonality, dual-approval policy norms).
revision_notes: Converted the case into an upbeat internal-audit annual report with detection-rate gains, a full-year timeline, and governance language. Buried the real flaws by presenting Q4 outperformance, dual sign-off, and 100% review of model-flagged entries as positive process details rather than overt threats to validity.

task_prompt:
To: Product Council
From: Payments Risk and Decisioning
Subject: Deployment proposal — next-generation card-not-present fraud ranking model

Payments Risk recommends deploying the new fraud ranking model to analyst review queues across our card-not-present portfolio. On the primary validation set, the model achieved a PR-AUC of 0.41 and dollar-weighted recall of 72% at the current review capacity threshold, outperforming the incumbent rules-plus-score stack on apparel, marketplace, and general merchandise traffic. Cross-category reporting remained directionally positive, and a time-based validation design inserted a 15-day gap between train and test windows to reduce temporal proximity leakage. Feature review also cleared standard card-not-present signals including IP geolocation, device reputation, velocity features, and merchant-behavior summaries.

Training data were drawn from e-commerce transactions issued by Issuer Bank over a 12-month period, with labels derived from Issuer Bank dispute resolution outcomes and confirmed-fraud investigations. The deployment recommendation extends beyond that initial training population to all card-not-present channels including travel, digital goods, and subscription services, on the view that these channels share enough online checkout structure for analyst triage use. The proposal is explicitly for ranking transactions for manual review, not for automatic declines, and score thresholds would remain under analyst-operations control.

To support generalization, the team ran a secondary validation on a sampled portfolio from a second issuer and observed directionally consistent lift in both PR-AUC and dollar-weighted recall. Product and Risk view that result as encouraging because the second issuer contributed different cardholders, merchant relationships, and dispute operations. Data quality review found no missing-label anomaly, and model documentation notes that dispute-based labels are the same operational truth source used in our current fraud workflow.

Given the measured gains, the cross-category readout, and the second-issuer check, the team believes the model is ready for deployment across the full card-not-present queue, including travel, digital goods, and subscription merchants. Please assess whether that recommendation is supported by the evidence as written, or whether the scope of the proposed claim still extends beyond what was actually validated.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The proposal has credible evidence for analyst triage in card-not-present settings similar to the evaluated issuer e-commerce portfolios, but recommending deployment across all card-not-present channels still exceeds the demonstrated scope.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a prospective segmented evaluation across additional card-not-present merchant categories such as travel, digital goods, and subscription services, ideally with multiple issuers using comparable label definitions.

planted_issues.description: The proposal moves from evidence on one issuer’s e-commerce portfolio to deployment across all card-not-present channels, including merchant categories with materially different fraud patterns.
Fraud labels come from Issuer Bank dispute-resolution outcomes, which are operationally standard but can differ across issuers and therefore limit how far the ranking claim generalizes.

ideal_critique: Recognize that the proposal is narrower than full auto-decisioning because it is about analyst triage, yet it still stretches beyond the training and validation scope by claiming readiness for all card-not-present channels.

Point out that travel, digital goods, and subscription services can have very different fraud signatures from the issuer’s core e-commerce mix, so cross-category evidence inside the original portfolio is not enough by itself.
Note that labels derived from one issuer’s dispute outcomes are reasonable but policy-dependent, which complicates generalization across issuers and merchant segments.
ideal_defense: Credit the team for several real strengths, including a leakage-aware temporal split, standard fraud features, and a second-issuer sample with directionally consistent results.
Defend the narrower claim that a ranking model for manual review is closer to the validated use than an automatic decline model would be.
Still argue that deployment language should be narrowed to card-not-present segments resembling the evaluated issuer portfolios unless broader segmented validation is completed.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Prospective merchant-segment audits show materially worse lift or calibration in travel, digital-goods, or financing categories than in the originally validated segments.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A prespecified rollout across the claimed merchant categories shows stable ranking performance and acceptable calibration for analyst triage across segments.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Performance remains strong in some additional merchant categories but degrades in others, making a narrower segmented claim appropriate.

must_not_claim: automatic_decline_decisioning_was_tested
time_split_validation_is_methodologically_invalid
the_15_day_gap_is_bad_practice_for_fraud_validation
using_ip_geolocation_features_is_inherently_improper_or_disqualifying

ground_truth_rationale: This stays mixed because the team has real evidence in its favor: strong PR-AUC and dollar-weighted recall, a proper temporal split with a gap, standard fraud signals, and a directionally consistent second-issuer sample. The buried problem is scope: those results do not automatically justify deployment across all card-not-present channels, especially where merchant mix and dispute-label policy differ.
notes: Medium mixed case. The evaluated use is somewhat aligned with the claim, but merchant-category and label-policy scope remain unresolved. Relabeled from medium to hard: single-pass assessor expected to fail ≥2 rubric dimensions.
revision_notes: Rewrote the case as a polished deployment proposal with strong fraud metrics, a leakage-aware validation design, standard feature review, and a second-issuer check. Buried the core overreach by placing the training scope in one paragraph and the recommendation for all card-not-present channels in another, while making issuer-specific dispute labels sound like ordinary data-quality detail.

task_prompt:
A city public-health team has built a neighborhood-week forecasting model for overdose spikes. The model combines wastewater measurements, EMS dispatch data, weather, mobility proxies, and recent overdose history to predict which neighborhoods will have unusually high overdose burden the following week. In retrospective evaluation over two years, the model showed useful ranking performance, and a spatial holdout analysis on neighborhoods omitted from training retained somewhat lower but still promising accuracy. The department wants to use the model to decide where to place a mobile naloxone and outreach van each week. A draft grant proposal says the system can direct van placement to reduce overdose deaths in urban neighborhoods.

Complicating the debate, the historical outcome labels are overdose deaths geocoded by the decedent’s residence, while the van would be sent to where overdoses are expected to occur and where people might accept services. During a one-month soft launch, the team informally sent the van to several high-score areas, but neighborhood partners differed in staffing, local trust, and available follow-up services, so outreach intensity was inconsistent and no counterfactual was maintained. Some staff argue that if the model can predict where burden will rise, it is already reasonable to say it can direct resources to reduce deaths, because targeting is the main policy decision. Others argue that the route from prediction to mortality reduction depends on service uptake, movement patterns, and the mismatch between residence-based labels and event-location operations. The model may be genuinely useful, but the dispute is whether the current claim is already justified, or whether a narrower claim or further study is needed before saying the system reduces overdose deaths.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The evidence may justify a narrower claim that the model helps prioritize neighborhoods for outreach, but it does not yet establish that model-guided van placement reduces overdose deaths.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: A stepped-wedge or randomized deployment that compares model-guided van placement against standard placement while measuring event-location coverage, service uptake, and overdose outcomes.

planted_issues.description: Predicting overdose burden does not by itself establish that model-guided outreach changes mortality outcomes.
The model is trained on residence-geocoded deaths, but the intervention targets event locations and transient populations, so the operational target differs from the training target.
The soft launch cannot attribute any benefit to the model because neighborhood staffing and partner capacity varied without a counterfactual.

ideal_critique: Notice both the intervention-effect problem and the subtler mismatch between residence-based labels and event-location deployment decisions.
Explain why the soft launch cannot establish outcome impact when service intensity and partner capacity differ by neighborhood.
Avoid dismissing the model entirely; it may still support prioritization even if mortality-reduction claims are premature.
ideal_defense: Defend the narrower operational claim that the model can help prioritize outreach locations, assuming the spatial holdout result is credible.
Concede that death-reduction language requires a controlled deployment study and better alignment between training target and operational target.
Frame the right next step as policy evaluation rather than claiming the current evidence already proves impact.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A stepped-wedge trial finds that model-guided van placement does not improve event-location coverage, naloxone distribution, or overdose outcomes relative to standard planning, or performs poorly when evaluated against event-location targets.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A prespecified controlled rollout shows that model-guided placement improves event-location coverage and service uptake and leads to lower overdose burden than standard planning.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model improves neighborhood prioritization metrics but the trial is underpowered on mortality or results depend strongly on local partner capacity.

must_not_claim: wastewater_data_are_inherently_invalid
spatial_holdout_is_evidence_of_data_leakage

ground_truth_rationale: This case is hard because the model could still be useful for prioritization even though the headline impact claim is unsupported. The label-target mismatch requires public-health reasoning beyond generic ML critique. The right resolution is to narrow the current claim and run a controlled policy evaluation.
notes: Hard mixed case with empirical-test resolution. Requires reasoning about operational target mismatch and intervention evaluation in public health. Domain expertise required: public health (overdose surveillance systems, outreach capacity constraints).

task_prompt: A retail chain ran a city-level ad experiment to estimate whether a new local ranking model for search ads increases in-store visits. Only 24 metropolitan areas were available for the test, and the treated and control cities are not perfectly balanced on raw baseline traffic by chance. A quick read could therefore conclude that the experiment is too small and too imbalanced to support any causal claim. The protocol is more careful than that. Before assignment, the analysts constructed 12 matched pairs of cities using the previous eight weeks of store-visit volume, search volume, and seasonality indicators. Within each pair, treatment was randomly assigned. Because the number of clusters is modest, the pre-analysis plan did not rely on large-sample asymptotic standard errors. Instead, it specified a randomization-inference test on the matched-pair assignment and a pair-weighted estimate of average lift. The final report uses exactly that design, shows the assignment mechanism, and presents the distribution of all possible within-pair treatment reassignments under the null. The estimated lift is positive and the randomization-inference p-value falls below the pre-specified threshold. The report also states that the result should be interpreted for large metro areas similar to those tested and not as a universal estimate for small rural markets. A critic could still focus on the low number of cities, the visible baseline imbalance in the aggregate table, or the absence of a standard regression p-value and claim the experiment is invalid. Exonerating the work requires noticing that the inference procedure was chosen for a small matched-cluster design, and that limited transportability beyond similar metros is a disclosure issue rather than a reason to reject the internal validity of the causal estimate.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the small-cluster experiment used prospectively defined matched-pair randomization and randomization inference appropriate to that design, with claims limited to similar metropolitan areas.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Verify the matched-pair assignment records and reproduce the pre-specified randomization-inference test over within-pair label permutations to confirm the reported p-value and pair-weighted lift estimate.

planted_issues.description: A critic may argue that 24 cities and imperfect aggregate balance invalidate the causal estimate, but that concern is invalid because matched-pair randomization and randomization inference were pre-specified precisely for this small-cluster setting.

ideal_critique: Check whether the matched-pair assignment and randomization-inference procedure were truly specified before outcomes were analyzed and whether the claim stays within similar metro areas.
A well-calibrated critique may note limited external validity to rural markets, but should not confuse that limitation with a failure of internal causal identification.
ideal_defense: Explain that with few clusters, randomization inference on the actual matched assignment is more appropriate than default large-sample regression approximations.
Point out that apparent aggregate imbalance is compatible with valid random assignment once the pair structure is respected.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Assignment departed from the documented matched-pair randomization, or the reported significance disappears when the pre-specified randomization-inference test is correctly reproduced.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Audit records confirm matched-pair random assignment and the reproduced randomization-inference test yields the reported significant lift within the tested metro population.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The sign of the effect is stable but the randomization-inference result is only borderline and highly sensitive to pair weighting choices.

must_not_claim: Any geo experiment with only 24 clusters is automatically invalid.
Visible imbalance in an aggregate baseline table proves randomization failure even when matching was used.
The absence of a standard asymptotic regression p-value means the study has no valid inference.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Only 24 geographic clusters; aggregate baseline imbalance despite matched randomization; use of randomization inference instead of a familiar large-sample regression p-value; claims limited to similar metro areas.
ground_truth_rationale: The challenge is separating a real limitation from a fatal flaw. Small-cluster geo experiments need design-aware inference, and this study uses a pre-specified matched-pair randomization plus randomization inference, so the causal claim within similar metros is methodologically sound even though generalization is limited.
notes: Hard causal-inference case requiring distinction between external-validity limits and internal-validity failure.

task_prompt: An e-commerce team wants to compare a new recommendation reranker against the current production policy using only logged historical interactions, so it uses an offline counterfactual evaluation rather than an immediate live experiment. The main estimator is self-normalized inverse propensity scoring with weight clipping at 20. Critics often view clipped IPS as suspicious because clipping introduces bias, and the setup can look even worse because some products were rarely shown under the historical policy. The report addresses this before presenting any model comparison. In a pre-analysis stage on older logs, the team checked overlap and found that the candidate reranker would only be evaluated on a restricted product shortlist where all items had non-negligible exposure under the logging policy. They also simulated variance-bias tradeoffs across clipping thresholds and selected a threshold of 20 before the final benchmark because unclipped IPS had unstable variance with occasional extreme weights, while clipping in the restricted-overlap regime changed estimates only modestly. The final report includes overlap diagnostics, effective sample size, and a sensitivity table for clipping thresholds 10, 20, and 50; the model ordering is stable across those settings. The claim is not that the exact effect size is unbiased in every possible catalog region. The narrower claim is that, on the supported shortlist with adequate overlap, the new reranker is preferred to baseline by standard off-policy estimators with stable diagnostics. A critic might still say that any clipped IPS estimate is invalid by construction. Exonerating the work requires recognizing that clipping can be a justified variance-control device when overlap is checked, support is restricted, sensitivity is reported, and the conclusion is correspondingly narrow.

ground_truth.final_verdict: There is no methodological issue because the clipped IPS evaluation is restricted to regions with adequate overlap, the clipping threshold was chosen in pre-analysis for variance control, and robustness diagnostics support the narrow off-policy comparison being claimed.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Verify overlap diagnostics and effective sample size on the evaluated shortlist, reproduce the pre-analysis that selected clipping threshold 20 before final comparison, and confirm that model ordering is stable across reasonable clipping thresholds.

planted_issues.description: A critic may say that clipping makes inverse propensity scoring invalid, but that concern is invalid here because support was restricted to adequate-overlap regions, the clip level was chosen prospectively for variance control, and sensitivity analyses show stable conclusions.

ideal_critique: Check whether overlap is actually sufficient on the evaluated shortlist and whether the reported conclusion stays within that supported region rather than overgeneralizing to poorly covered catalog areas.

A calibrated critique may note that clipping introduces bias and therefore exact effect magnitudes should be interpreted cautiously, but that does not automatically overturn a stable model-ranking conclusion under adequate overlap.
ideal_defense: Explain that off-policy evaluation in sparse recommendation logs requires a variance-bias tradeoff, and that clipping is acceptable when support restrictions and sensitivity analyses are explicit.
Point to the pre-analysis selecting the clip threshold, the overlap diagnostics, and the stable ordering across multiple clip levels.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Effective sample size is too low, overlap diagnostics fail on the evaluated shortlist, or model ranking changes materially across reasonable clipping thresholds.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The evaluated shortlist shows adequate overlap and usable effective sample size, the clip threshold was chosen before the final benchmark, and model ordering is stable across sensitivity analyses.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The preferred model stays ahead at clip 20 but rankings compress or partially reverse at nearby clip thresholds, leaving uncertainty about whether the conclusion is robust enough.

must_not_claim: Any use of clipped IPS makes off-policy recommendation evaluation invalid.
Because clipping is biased, the benchmark cannot support even a narrow model-ranking conclusion.
Offline evaluation is unsound unless the candidate policy has full support over the entire catalog rather than over the explicitly evaluated shortlist.

suspicious_but_valid_features: Use of clipped self-normalized IPS; evaluation limited to a shortlist with documented support overlap; sensitivity tables rather than a single estimator; conclusion framed as a narrow supported-region ranking claim rather than a universal unbiased effect estimate.
ground_truth_rationale: This case hinges on calibration about off-policy evaluation. Clipping is not automatically fatal when it is prospectively chosen, overlap is adequate, and the claim is limited to a supported region with sensitivity checks demonstrating stable conclusions.
notes: Hard recommendation-systems case requiring nuanced understanding of overlap, clipping, and narrow causal claims.

task_prompt:
A mid-sized bank wants to expand a machine-learning underwriting model for unsecured personal loans to applicants with variable gig-income histories. The credit policy team currently relies on bureau scores plus manual review for many of these applicants, which slows turnaround and limits volume. Model developers present a retrospective validation on three years of prior applications. Among applicants who were approved and booked, the model predicts 12-month serious delinquency substantially better than the bank's scorecard and appears especially strong on self-employed and platform-worker segments. Executives are considering using the model to approve some borderline applicants automatically and to decline others without manual review.

The historical data include application features, bank transaction summaries for customers who linked accounts, and repayment outcomes for booked loans. For declined applications, there is no repayment label. The validation deck shows calibration plots and profitability estimates based on historical loss assumptions, and it notes that the model was trained on a period with low unemployment and relatively stable household cash flows. Compliance staff point out that the bank would still run standard fair-lending reviews before launch. Some committee members worry that using linked-account cash-flow features could create noisy or seasonal signals, but the modeling team says those features materially improve ranking.

The lending committee asks whether the retrospective validation is enough to justify deploying the model for prospective approval and decline decisions in this new applicant segment. Consider both statistical performance and the framing of the real production decision.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective validation is insufficient for deployment on its own, but a well-designed champion-challenger pilot could resolve whether the model improves decisions for previously unseen applicant segments.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Conduct a monitored champion-challenger pilot with manual override retention, measuring realized loss-adjusted return and fair-lending metrics over 12 months for newly approved and declined applicant bands.

planted_issues.description: Outcomes are only observed for booked loans, so the bank cannot directly validate prospective decline decisions on previously rejected applicants.
Training and validation from a benign credit period may not reflect default behavior in a weaker economy, especially for variable-income borrowers.
The deployment creates different costs for false approvals and false denials, but the evidence emphasizes ranking and historical profitability rather than threshold-specific decision tradeoffs.

ideal_critique: Identify reject inference as central because the bank wants to automate declines, not just rank already booked loans.
Note that a low-unemployment backtest may not transport to the intended deployment period or segment.
Ask for threshold-level evidence tied to approval, decline, and manual-review decisions.
ideal_defense: Concede that the model may be useful for prioritizing manual review even if full auto-decline is not justified.
Defend fair-lending review and account-linkage features as not, by themselves, evidence of invalidity.
Argue for a constrained rollout with monitored approval bands rather than immediate full automation.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A champion-challenger pilot shows that applicants the model would newly decline but humans would have approved do not exhibit meaningfully worse delinquency than the approved pool after 12 months, or that performance degrades materially in a rising-unemployment cohort.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a monitored pilot with manual overrides retained, the model's approve and review bands improve realized loss-adjusted return without worsening fair-lending metrics and newly approved applicants perform within preset delinquency limits over 12 months.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Short-horizon pilot results look favorable for approvals but there is still no credible evidence about long-run outcomes of applicants the model would decline.

must_not_claim: Using linked-account cash-flow data is automatically illegal or unfair.
Seasonal income patterns alone prove the model is invalid.

ground_truth_rationale: Because the deployment decision includes automated declines, observed outcomes only on booked loans create a major evidence gap. The benign macro period and unstated threshold tradeoffs further weaken the claim that retrospective performance justifies prospective underwriting decisions.
notes: Medium case. Lending domain. Retrospective evaluation used to justify prospective deployment. Red herring: linked-account features may look suspicious but are not automatically invalid. Added empirical_test_agreed as secondary resolution. Upgraded to mixed: defense has a genuine argument that a pilot study could validate the approach for the unseen population. Relabeled from medium to hard: single-pass assessor expected to fail ≥2 rubric dimensions.

task_prompt:
A large property insurer wants to deploy a computer-vision model that triages roof-damage claims after hailstorms. The current process sends a field adjuster to most claims, which creates long delays after major weather events. The proposed workflow would automatically route low-risk claims to desk review with satellite imagery and contractor photos, while reserving scarce field adjusters for claims the model predicts are likely to involve structural damage or fraud indicators. The vendor presents retrospective results on 40,000 prior claims and says the model could reduce field inspections by nearly half.

The labels used in training come from final claim dispositions and estimated repair severity in the insurer's internal system. However, field inspections were historically assigned partly by simple rules such as roof age, claim amount, region, and whether the first notice of loss mentioned interior water. Claims sent only to desk review often received less extensive documentation. The retrospective validation compares model scores to final severity labels and reports strong precision at the threshold selected for desk-review routing. It does not separately report outcomes for catastrophe periods, when independent adjusters and emergency tarping vendors are heavily involved. The operations team also notes that the model uses different photo sources in production than in some historical claims, because new self-service upload tools were introduced this year.

Claims leadership asks whether the backtest is enough to justify routing some future roof claims away from field inspection. Consider the production decision and the kinds of mistakes the insurer most needs to avoid.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The backtest is not sufficient for deployment because historical inspection assignment and documentation practices confound the labels and the evaluation understates the cost of missing serious claims.

planted_issues.description: Historical severity labels depend partly on which claims received field inspections, so the model may learn prior triage policy rather than true latent damage severity.
Desk-reviewed claims often had less evidence collected, making severity outcomes not directly comparable across historical pathways.
The intended deployment is especially valuable during catastrophe surges and with new photo-upload channels, but those conditions are not well validated in the retrospective study.

ideal_critique: Observe that historical field-inspection assignment affects both what evidence exists and what final severity was recorded.
Explain why missing a truly severe roof claim is costlier than sending an unnecessary field adjuster, so threshold metrics must match that tradeoff.
Ask for validation on catastrophe-period claims and new self-service image inputs.
ideal_defense: Concede that the model may still help prioritize adjusters without being the sole routing authority.
Defend use of internal claim outcomes as a starting point for development, while acknowledging they are not a deployment-grade target by themselves.
Suggest a controlled rollout with audit reinspections of desk-reviewed claims.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a pilot with random audit field inspections of desk-reviewed claims, the missed severe-damage rate exceeds the preset claims-risk tolerance or is materially higher during catastrophe periods than in the retrospective estimate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a pilot with audit reinspections, desk-review routing reduces field visits by at least 25% while keeping missed severe-damage findings below the preset tolerance across both routine and catastrophe claims.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Routine-period audit results look acceptable but catastrophe-period volume is too small to determine whether the routing policy is safe during the very surges it is meant to address.

must_not_claim: Using contractor photos is inherently invalid evidence.
Regional variation in hail frequency alone makes the model unusable everywhere.

ground_truth_rationale: This deployment decides which claims lose a field inspection, so historical pathway-dependent labels are a serious problem. The model may still have operational value, but the backtest does not directly establish safety for routing future claims away from in-person assessment.
notes: Medium case. Insurance claims triage domain. Relabeled from medium to hard: single-pass assessor expected to fail ≥2 rubric dimensions.

task_prompt:
A children's hospital is considering deploying an early-deterioration model on its general pediatric wards. The score would run continuously and page the rapid-response nurse when a patient crosses threshold twice within an hour. Hospital leaders are interested because several recent safety reviews found delayed escalation before some ICU transfers. The data science team presents a retrospective validation on four years of ward admissions showing substantially better discrimination than the hospital's bedside warning score for the endpoint of ICU transfer or ward cardiac arrest within the next 12 hours. A neighboring hospital in the same network reports similar retrospective performance.

The proposed deployment is advisory rather than fully automated, but a page from the system is expected to trigger an in-person assessment and could influence when residents call attendings. Historical training features include vital signs, oxygen settings, nursing documentation, and medication administrations. During the study period, however, some units already used proactive rapid-response rounding for high-risk patients, and clinicians occasionally transferred patients early based on concern before documented vital-sign deterioration. Patients with complex chronic conditions were overrepresented in the retrospective positives. The evaluation emphasizes AUROC and lead time, with a brief sensitivity analysis by service line, but it does not estimate how many pages per shift the chosen threshold would create or whether alerts would remain predictive once they begin changing clinician behavior. The hospital also plans to suppress pages for patients on end-of-life pathways and for those already receiving one-to-one ICU outreach, which a few committee members view as suspicious customization.

The safety committee asks whether the evidence is strong enough to turn on paging, whether only a silent phase is justified, or whether the model should not move forward at all. Consider the clinical deployment context rather than just the retrospective ranking result.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The model is promising but retrospective evidence alone is not enough for paging deployment; the right next step is a prospective silent run or staged trial with explicit safety thresholds.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a prospective silent trial followed by a staged or stepped-wedge ward deployment measuring page burden, clinician response behavior, ICU transfers, and adverse events against prespecified non-inferiority or benefit criteria.

planted_issues.description: ICU transfer within 12 hours is partly shaped by prior clinician concern and rapid-response practices, so retrospective labels are affected by the care process the model would change.
The proposed action is repeated paging, but the evaluation does not quantify threshold-specific alert volume or downstream alarm fatigue.
Retrospective positives are concentrated in complex chronic patients, raising uncertainty about calibration and actionability across broader ward populations.
Suppression for end-of-life and ICU-outreach patients may be sensible, but the chosen exclusions alter the deployment population and need prospective validation.

ideal_critique: Notice that the outcome is treatment-sensitive: clinician concern and proactive transfers change whether the label occurs.
Emphasize that paging burden is part of the intervention and must be measured directly before go-live.
Ask whether apparent gains are concentrated in patient groups already receiving intensive monitoring.
ideal_defense: Concede that retrospective AUROC is not enough for paging deployment, but argue the evidence is strong enough to justify a silent run or staged trial rather than abandonment.
Defend suppression of end-of-life and ICU-outreach patients as clinically plausible workflow alignment, not a fatal flaw.
Use the neighboring hospital replication as supportive but not dispositive evidence.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a silent run and staged rollout, the chosen threshold would generate page volumes above ward staffing limits, or alerts fail to improve escalation timeliness or adverse-event rates once clinician behavior and suppression rules are accounted for.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a silent run and staged rollout, page burden remains within preset staffing limits and alerted patients show improved escalation timeliness or fewer unexpected ICU transfers without excess unnecessary assessments.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model enriches for deterioration in silent mode, but deployment changes clinician behavior enough that patient-outcome benefit and acceptable page burden remain uncertain.

must_not_claim: Suppressing end-of-life or ICU-outreach patients automatically invalidates the model.
Any use of medication administrations in the model is necessarily leakage.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a hard mixed case because the retrospective evidence is genuinely suggestive, yet the labels and intervention are entangled with clinical workflow. In pediatric deterioration deployment, paging burden and treatment-sensitive outcomes require prospective clinical validation rather than immediate acceptance or categorical rejection.
notes: Hard clinical deployment case requiring domain knowledge about alarm fatigue and treatment-affected outcomes. Two interacting high-severity issues. Red herring: suppression rules may look suspicious but can be clinically appropriate. Domain expertise required: clinical workflow (alarm fatigue, pediatric early warning score protocols).

task_prompt:
A global bank's financial-crime unit wants to deploy a machine-learning system that suppresses some low-risk anti-money-laundering alerts before they reach human investigators. Today, a rules engine generates a very large queue of transaction-monitoring alerts, and analysts close most of them as non-suspicious after brief review. The proposed model would sit after the rules engine and automatically close the lowest-risk portion of alerts, while sending the rest through the usual investigative workflow. The vendor shows retrospective results on two years of historical alerts, claiming the model could reduce analyst workload by 40% while preserving nearly all suspicious activity report (SAR) filings.

Training labels are based on final investigator dispositions, escalation decisions, and whether an alert family eventually led to a SAR. During the study period, alert-handling practices changed after an enforcement action: documentation requirements increased, some scenarios were re-tuned, and investigators were instructed to escalate more aggressively for certain jurisdictions. The model was validated by comparing suppression candidates against eventual SAR linkage and investigator close rates. Compliance leaders note that the bank would still keep sanctions screening, politically exposed person reviews, and high-risk geography rules unchanged. Some executives argue that because the model only acts after the existing rules engine, any false negative risk is limited. Others point out that the highest-risk cost is missing suspicious activity that never gets investigated deeply enough to be linked to a SAR.

The model risk committee asks whether the retrospective evidence is sufficient to deploy automated alert suppression, whether only a narrow pilot is defensible, or whether the approach is not supportable in this setting. Consider both ML evidence and the financial-crime deployment context.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The system may be defensible only through a tightly controlled parallel-run pilot, because retrospective agreement with past alert handling is not enough to justify automated AML alert suppression.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run the model in parallel without suppression, require independent second-look review on sampled would-suppress alerts, and compare missed suspicious activity, SAR outcomes, and validation artifacts against preset compliance tolerances.

planted_issues.description: Historical close and escalate labels reflect prior investigative policy and enforcement pressure, not a stable ground truth of true suspiciousness.
Using eventual SAR linkage as a target misses suspicious activity that was never investigated deeply enough to generate a filing.
For AML alert suppression, governance and model validation generally require scenario analysis, tuning justification, and evidence beyond retrospective workload and agreement metrics.
Rule retuning and post-enforcement workflow changes mean the historical alert stream is not a stable proxy for the future operating environment.

ideal_critique: Identify that both investigator disposition and SAR linkage are policy-contaminated outcome proxies in AML operations.
Bring in domain knowledge: suppressing alerts has regulatory validation expectations beyond ordinary retrospective model metrics.
Push for a parallel-run design with independent review of would-suppress alerts rather than immediate automation.
ideal_defense: Concede that full automated suppression is not justified by this backtest alone.
Defend keeping sanctions, PEP, and high-risk geography controls unchanged as a meaningful guardrail rather than proof of invalidity.
Argue that if a pilot shows extremely low miss rates under independent review, selective suppression could still be operationally reasonable.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: During a parallel-run, the model-assisted pipeline misses at least one sanctions-relevant alert that the rule-based system caught, or the false positive reduction is less than 10%, indicating no meaningful improvement over the existing system.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A 6-month parallel-run with blinded human reviewers shows the model-assisted pipeline detects at least 95% of cases flagged by the existing rule-based system, with false positive rate reduced by at least 20%, and no sanctions-relevant alerts are missed.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model catches all sanctions alerts but false positive reduction is between 10-20%, making the operational benefit marginal relative to the regulatory risk of the transition.

must_not_claim: Because the model acts after rules, false negatives are impossible.
Any use of SAR-related outcomes in model development is automatically prohibited.

ground_truth_rationale: This case is mixed because the bank may be able to justify a constrained pilot, but not direct suppression deployment, and the right answer depends on compliance-grade parallel validation. It is hard because resolving it requires financial-crime domain knowledge about policy-driven labels, incomplete SAR proxies, and model-governance expectations.
notes: Hard AML deployment case requiring regulatory and model-risk expertise. Multiple interacting high-severity issues. Red herring: preserving sanctions/PEP/high-risk rules is a real guardrail, not itself a flaw. Domain expertise required: AML/regulatory (SAR filing standards, regulatory validation requirements).

task_prompt:
A state wildfire agency is evaluating a vision model that detects new smoke plumes from mountaintop camera feeds. The agency is considering using the model to reduce overnight staffing at a regional monitoring center by having one dispatcher supervise multiple camera districts, with human escalation only when the model or a neighboring center flags likely smoke. The vendor presents a retrospective study from the last two fire seasons showing high frame-level detection and faster average time-to-flag than manual monitoring on archived incidents. Budget pressure makes the staffing change attractive before the next dry season.

The historical evaluation used confirmed wildfire and prescribed-burn incident logs to label positive windows on archived camera feeds. Small ignitions that self-extinguished, were obscured by weather, or were never officially logged are not represented directly. Cameras were upgraded during the study period, and some districts now have different vegetation patterns, tourism traffic, and construction haze than they did in the retrospective set. The agency's proposed operations concept assumes that when the model stays silent, the reduced overnight team will not watch every feed continuously. The validation deck reports false-positive rates per camera-hour and event-level recall for logged incidents, but it does not estimate how often model silence would delay first awareness compared with current staffing on marginal overnight starts. Some managers are focused on the model's variable performance near sunrise and sunset, while others point out that those periods are already handled by shift overlap and may not drive the main risk.

Agency leadership asks whether the retrospective evidence is sufficient to justify staffing changes tied to model monitoring. Consider the decision framing, the missing counterfactuals, and the operational asymmetry between missed and extra alerts.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective incident study does not justify reducing overnight human monitoring because it cannot establish the miss risk created when model silence replaces continuous human attention.

planted_issues.description: Using only confirmed incident logs as positives omits small or unreported ignitions, so retrospective recall does not capture the true miss risk of replacing human vigilance.
The proposed deployment uses model silence to justify fewer humans watching feeds, making the relevant counterfactual different from retrospective frame scoring on archived incidents.
Camera upgrades and changed environmental conditions create prospective shift relative to the archived study period.
Missing a real overnight ignition can be far more costly than investigating extra false smoke alerts, but the staffing proposal is justified mainly with average detection metrics.

ideal_critique: Recognize that the deployment decision is really about reducing human watch coverage when the model is silent, not merely detecting smoke on archived data.
Highlight the unobserved-positive problem: logged incidents are an incomplete proxy for all starts that matter operationally.
Stress that the cost of a missed overnight ignition is highly asymmetric relative to nuisance alerts.
ideal_defense: Concede that staffing reduction is harder to justify than using the model as an extra detection aid.
Defend shift-overlap handling of sunrise and sunset as a plausible non-issue if those periods are outside the proposed unattended window.
Suggest using the model to augment, not replace, overnight monitoring until parallel operational evidence exists.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a parallel season where current staffing is maintained, simulated reduced-watch operations based on model silence would have delayed first awareness beyond the agency's preset tolerance for any meaningful number of overnight ignitions or near-miss starts.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a parallel season with maintained staffing, simulated reduced-watch operations show no delays beyond the preset tolerance on logged and independently adjudicated near-miss overnight starts while materially lowering nuisance-monitoring burden.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The model performs well on logged fires, but the agency cannot credibly estimate how often silent periods would miss small or transient overnight ignitions that humans might have noticed.

must_not_claim: Poor sunrise or sunset performance alone is enough to reject the staffing proposal.
Any use of prescribed burns in evaluation automatically contaminates the dataset.

ground_truth_rationale: This is a hard deployment-framing case because the agency wants to remove human monitoring capacity when the model is silent. Archived detection on logged incidents does not answer the relevant safety question, especially when unlogged starts and extreme miss costs dominate the decision.
notes: Hard wildfire detection and staffing case. Multiple interacting high-severity issues. Red herring: sunrise/sunset variability may look central but is plausibly handled by shift overlap. Domain expertise required: emergency management (wildfire detection asymmetric error costs, staffing counterfactuals).

task_prompt:
A large e-commerce marketplace is deciding whether to replace its search ranking model for product search. The current production stack is a gradient-boosted reranker using handcrafted lexical and behavioral features. The proposed replacement is a transformer-based ranker that consumes the query text, product title, and catalog attributes directly. In the project review, the search team reports that the new model improves offline NDCG@10 by 6.3% and improves add-to-cart rate on the top ten results by 4.1% in replay evaluation, and they describe the result as evidence that the ranking model is better.

The appendix shows that the baseline number was copied from the search dashboard used in early October, when the company tracked desktop web searches and excluded brand-name queries because those were treated as navigational. The challenger was evaluated on the most recent four weeks of traffic after a mobile-app redesign placed the search box on the home screen, which increased mobile traffic and changed the mix toward more brand and long-tail queries. The challenger replay also used a newer query-normalization service that expands abbreviations and unit expressions before retrieval and feature generation, while the baseline replay reused the older raw logged query text because the previous normalization output was not stored. The team notes that catalog size increased for the holiday assortment between the two periods, but each replay used the items that were actually active at the time of each query.

Supporters of the launch argue that the improvement is probably real because a quick check on the 1,000 most frequent non-brand queries still showed a smaller positive gain, and the retrieval stack and business rules were otherwise unchanged. Critics argue the benchmark is not apples to apples. Assess the benchmark design and what test would settle the disagreement.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The reported lift is not a fair baseline comparison yet, but the new search ranker could still be genuinely better once both systems are re-evaluated under the same query mix and preprocessing rules.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Rebuild one shared holdout of queries from the same date range, run both rankers with the same query-normalization and retrieval pipeline, include the same query types for both systems, and compare NDCG@10 and add-to-cart@10 overall and by brand versus generic, head versus tail, and desktop versus mobile segments.

planted_issues.description: The baseline and challenger were evaluated on different traffic periods and inclusion rules, so the query distribution differs in ways that can change ranking metrics even if model quality did not.
Only the challenger benefited from the newer query-normalization step, so part of the reported gain may come from preprocessing rather than the ranking model itself.

ideal_critique: Notice that the baseline and challenger were not scored on the same query population because the period, platform mix, and brand-query inclusion changed.
Call out that query normalization is part of the evaluation pipeline, so applying it only to the challenger breaks attribution to the model.
Ask for a rerun on a shared holdout with identical preprocessing and segment-level reporting rather than accepting the headline lift at face value.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current benchmark does not isolate model quality cleanly enough to support a definitive replacement decision.
Argue that the positive spot check on frequent generic queries makes it plausible that at least some true model gain exists.
Maintain that the right next step is a corrected re-evaluation, not automatic dismissal of the new model.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: On the shared holdout with matched normalization and inclusion rules, the challenger's NDCG@10 gain falls below 1% relative or add-to-cart@10 is not positive in most major query segments.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: On the shared holdout with matched normalization and inclusion rules, the challenger improves NDCG@10 by at least 3% relative and add-to-cart@10 by at least 2% relative overall, with positive results in both generic and non-brand mobile and desktop segments.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The corrected rerun shows a small overall gain between 1% and 3% relative or positive results only in some segments such as tail queries while other major segments are flat.

must_not_claim: Using a recent time-based holdout is automatically leakage in search evaluation.
Brand queries must always be excluded from ranking benchmarks regardless of the product question.

ground_truth_rationale: The critique is legitimate because the reported comparison changes both the evaluated query mix and the preprocessing pipeline, so the headline lift cannot be cleanly attributed to the new ranker. The defense is also legitimate because nothing in the scenario proves the gain is fake, and the smaller positive result on a restricted non-brand slice makes a real improvement plausible. A controlled rerun on matched queries and preprocessing would directly resolve the disagreement.
notes: Red herring feature: holiday catalog growth looks suspicious, but the replay used the items active at each query time, so catalog expansion alone is not the benchmark flaw. Must-find issues are intentionally independent: one is about changed query distribution, the other about unequal preprocessing.

task_prompt:
An online learning platform is reviewing a new recommendation model that chooses each student's next algebra activity. The current system mostly recommends the next lesson in a fixed curriculum path. The proposed model uses recent mistakes, pace, and confidence estimates to choose among practice sets, short videos, and review checks. In the rollout memo, the team says the model improves learning outcomes because students exposed to it completed 14% more recommended activities, spent 11% more minutes practicing, and were 7% more likely to keep a seven-day learning streak during a four-week experiment.

The details section explains that the new policy often recommends shorter skill checks and recap videos when the model predicts confusion, so the average recommended activity length fell from about 11 minutes to 7 minutes. The platform does have end-of-unit mastery quizzes and a weekly mixed-topic review meant to test cumulative understanding, but only 38% of active students reached a mastery quiz within the experiment window, so the headline deck omitted those outcomes. On that subset, mastery-quiz scores were about 1.8 percentage points higher under the new model, but the interval was wide enough that the team described it as directional rather than conclusive. The report also does not include any delayed retention measure, such as whether students still solved similar problems one or two weeks later without hints.

Product managers argue that more practice and higher persistence are reasonable proxies for learning, especially because the early quiz readout was at least not negative. Critics respond that the team promised better learning outcomes, not just more clicks or more short tasks. Evaluate whether the current evidence supports the claim and what study would settle it.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The evidence supports that the recommender increased engagement, but it does not yet show that students learned more unless a direct mastery and retention study confirms the proxy metrics.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a randomized comparison in which both policies are evaluated on the same downstream common assessment: immediate end-of-unit mastery quiz performance and a delayed mixed-topic retention test one to two weeks later, while also reporting the difficulty and length distribution of assigned activities under each policy.

planted_issues.description: The headline metrics are completion, minutes, and streaks, which measure engagement rather than the claimed outcome of improved learning or mastery.
Because the new policy recommends shorter activities, higher completion counts can rise even if actual learning per student does not improve.
The study does not test whether any gain persists on delayed or mixed-topic assessments, so the claim about learning durability is unproven.

ideal_critique: Separate the team's engagement evidence from its stronger claim about learning outcomes.
Notice that completion becomes easier to improve when the recommended activities are shorter, so the reported proxy is partly protocol-sensitive.
Ask for direct mastery and delayed retention outcomes on common assessments rather than relying on streaks and minutes alone.
ideal_defense: Concede that engagement metrics are only proxies and cannot by themselves prove better learning.
Argue that more practice and persistence are still meaningful leading indicators, especially because the early quiz subset was directionally positive rather than contradictory.
Frame the current evidence as sufficient to justify a stronger learning study, not as a finished proof of improved mastery.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In the randomized follow-up, the new policy shows no improvement of at least 2 percentage points on the common mastery quiz and no improvement on the delayed retention test after matching for activity difficulty and length.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In the randomized follow-up, students under the new policy score at least 3 percentage points higher on the common mastery quiz and at least 3 percentage points higher on the delayed retention test, with benefits remaining after controlling for activity difficulty and length.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The new policy improves immediate quiz scores but not delayed retention, or the gains appear only in specific student subgroups while the overall average effect stays small.

must_not_claim: Completion and practice-time metrics are never useful in education experiments.
Because only a subset reached mastery quizzes, the quiz results are automatically meaningless.

ground_truth_rationale: The critique is legitimate because the team claims better learning outcomes while mostly reporting engagement metrics that can move for reasons unrelated to mastery, including shorter assigned activities. The defense also has a real argument because engagement can be a useful proxy and the limited quiz evidence is at least directionally supportive rather than obviously inconsistent. A randomized study with common immediate and delayed assessments would decide whether the proxy improvements translate into learning gains.
notes: Red herring feature: only students who reached sufficient progress could take the mastery quiz, which may sound fatal, but subset quiz evidence can still be informative if interpreted cautiously. Must-find issues are independent: proxy mismatch, shorter-content inflation, and missing retention measurement can each be identified separately.

task_prompt:
A manufacturing group is reviewing a machine-learning system that predicts which printed circuit boards should receive an extra X-ray inspection before final electrical test. The existing process inspects boards according to fixed rules based on product family and occasional operator escalation. The proposed model uses machine telemetry, solder-paste inspection images, and placement history to prioritize boards that are most likely to contain hidden solder defects.
