In the project summary, the team reports that final-test defect rate on Surface-Mount Line 3 fell from 2.4% in the six weeks before launch to 1.5% in the six weeks after launch, and they present that drop as evidence that the model reduced defects.

The operations log notes two other changes that happened around the same time. First, maintenance crews completed a scheduled stencil replacement and reflow-oven recalibration on Line 3 three days before the model went live. Second, during the launch window the plant temporarily shifted a complex controller-board family to another site, so Line 3 produced a higher share of simpler two-layer boards than in the earlier six-week period. The team says inspector headcount, acceptance thresholds, and the budget for extra X-ray checks stayed the same. They also report that boards flagged by the model were materially more likely to fail downstream inspection than boards selected by the old rules, which supporters cite as evidence that the model is adding value even if the overall defect drop has other contributors. The vision backbone had been pretrained on archived plant images from the prior year.

Assess whether the reported defect-rate improvement is already convincing and what comparison would resolve the dispute.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The before-after defect-rate drop is confounded by concurrent process and product-mix changes, but the model may still add real inspection value that a controlled same-period comparison could verify.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: After maintenance has stabilized, run a same-period controlled pilot on matched board families and shifts, randomly or alternately assigning model-assisted versus standard inspection under the same X-ray budget, and compare final-test defect rate, true defects found per inspected board, and false-reject rate.

planted_issues.description: Stencil replacement and oven recalibration occurred just before launch, so part of the defect-rate drop could come from process improvement rather than the model.
The launch period contained a simpler SKU mix, which can reduce defect rates even if inspection quality is unchanged.
Because the evidence is a before-after comparison on one line without a same-period control, the model effect cannot be separated from other operational changes.

ideal_critique: Connect the launch timing to the scheduled maintenance work and recognize that recalibration alone could lower defects.
Notice that the mix shifted toward simpler boards, which independently changes expected defect rates.
Ask for a same-period controlled comparison on matched products and shifts instead of relying on one line's before-after trend.
ideal_defense: Concede that the plant-wide defect-rate drop cannot be treated as a clean causal estimate of model impact.
Argue that the stronger downstream hit rate among model-flagged boards is still meaningful evidence that the model may improve targeting within a fixed inspection budget.
Support a controlled pilot rather than claiming the current observational result is already definitive.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In the controlled same-period pilot, model-assisted inspection reduces final-test defect rate by less than 0.2 percentage points versus standard inspection or fails to increase true defects found per inspected board.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In the controlled same-period pilot, model-assisted inspection lowers final-test defect rate by at least 0.6 percentage points and increases true defects found per inspected board by at least 20% without increasing false rejects by more than 0.1 percentage points.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The controlled pilot shows a modest improvement in inspection targeting but only a small or inconsistent reduction in final-test defects across board families or shifts.

must_not_claim: Pretraining on archived plant images automatically leaks the test labels.
Keeping inspector headcount and acceptance thresholds fixed is itself a methodological flaw.

ground_truth_rationale: The critique is legitimate because two operational changes that affect defect rates were present right at launch, and the design lacks a contemporaneous control to separate them from model impact. The defense is also legitimate because the higher yield among model-flagged boards suggests the model may still be improving inspection prioritization even if the overall before-after drop is overstated. A controlled same-period pilot would distinguish true model value from plant changes.
notes: Red herring feature: pretraining the vision backbone on archived plant images may sound suspicious, but it is not the causal flaw in this case. The planted issues are intentionally non-interacting: maintenance timing, SKU mix, and absence of a same-period control can each be spotted on their own.

task_prompt:
A parcel-delivery company is evaluating a route-optimization model for urban same-day deliveries. Dispatchers currently assign stops to drivers by territory and use a heuristic route planner, with manual edits for traffic, building access, and late-arriving orders. The proposed model jointly chooses stop order and route adjustments within each driver's territory. In a retrospective review across 14 depots, the data science team reports that replaying last quarter's delivery manifests through the optimizer would have reduced driven miles by 9% while keeping the historical on-time-delivery rate essentially unchanged. Operations leaders are treating the result as evidence that the system is ready for deployment.

The simulation assumes the optimizer receives the full list of stops for the day at 8:00 a.m. and then computes one recommended route plan per driver. In actual operations, roughly 22% of same-day orders are added after 8:00 a.m., and dispatchers insert them throughout the day. The replay also scores the optimizer using the actual service times and travel times observed on the historical routes, including apartment access delays, parking luck, and temporary road conditions that would not be known perfectly at dispatch time. Drivers in production are allowed to ignore suggested resequencing when building access rules or customer requests conflict with the app. Supporters argue that the benchmark is still informative because about 70% of daily volume is known by 8:00 a.m., driver territories stay fixed, and the optimizer looked especially strong on dense downtown routes where stop order matters most.

Assess whether the retrospective evidence already supports deployment readiness and what test would fairly settle the disagreement.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The retrospective replay is informative but not deployment-ready proof, because it assumes a cleaner and more fully known routing problem than the one the live system will face.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a shadow-mode or limited live depot pilot in which the optimizer uses only information available at decision time, handles late-order insertions, records driver overrides, and is judged on actual miles driven, on-time rate, and dispatcher intervention versus the incumbent routing workflow.

planted_issues.description: The replay gives the optimizer the entire day's stop list at 8:00 a.m., but live dispatch must absorb a substantial share of orders that arrive later.
The evaluation scores routes using realized service and travel times that would only be forecast at dispatch time, so the simulation is more informed than deployment.
The retrospective study does not measure how often drivers and dispatchers would override suggested routes, even though live compliance is part of the real system outcome.

ideal_critique: Notice that the replay problem is more static than the live one because it assumes the full manifest is known at the morning dispatch decision.
Call out that realized travel and service times make the offline simulation more favorable than a forecast-driven production setting.
Ask for a shadow or limited live pilot that measures actual route outcomes including late inserts and override rates.
ideal_defense: Concede that the current replay does not fully replicate live dispatch conditions and therefore is not sufficient for a strong deployment-readiness claim.
Argue that the replay still provides useful evidence because most volume is known early and the optimizer may genuinely help on the static portion of the problem.
Support moving to a constrained pilot rather than treating the retrospective result as final proof.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: In a shadow or limited live pilot using only real-time available information, the optimizer reduces miles by less than 2% or causes on-time delivery to worsen by more than 1 percentage point once late orders and overrides are included.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: In a shadow or limited live pilot using only real-time available information, the optimizer reduces miles by at least 5% with on-time delivery changing by no more than 0.5 percentage points and driver override rates staying below 15% of stops.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The live pilot improves miles on early-known routes but loses most of the benefit after late-order insertions or shows strong depot-to-depot variation in override rates and on-time performance.

must_not_claim: Any retrospective routing evaluation is useless and provides no information at all.
Fixed driver territories automatically make route optimization invalid.

ground_truth_rationale: The critique is legitimate because the replay bakes in conditions that are easier than live deployment, especially full early knowledge of stops and realized rather than forecasted timing inputs. The defense is also legitimate because retrospective replay can still be meaningfully suggestive for the large static portion of the routing problem, particularly on dense routes. A pilot under real dispatch constraints would reveal whether the offline advantage survives contact with operations.
notes: Red herring feature: fixed driver territories may look like an outdated operational constraint, but they do not invalidate the benchmark by themselves. The planted issues are intentionally separable: manifest completeness, realized timing inputs, and live override behavior are each discoverable without first finding the others.
